products.description: Unitree G1-D Standard A U1 Fixed Base Dual-Arm Wheeled Humanoid Robot (Coming Soon) The G1-D Standard A U1 is configured with two Dex1-1 force-controlled two-finger grippers as its standard end-effectors — making it the entry-level manipulation configuration in the G1-D Standard lineup, optimized for general pick-and-place, object transport, and straightforward grasping tasks that do not require three-finger or five-finger dexterity. Key confirmed specifications include: 17 total degrees of freedom (excluding end effectors: 7 DOF × 2 arms + 2 DOF waist + 1 DOF column = 17), height adjustable from 1,260 to 1,680 mm with a 500 mm vertical lifting stroke at 1 mm precision, 3 kg maximum single-arm payload, 450 mm arm reach (excluding end effector), NVIDIA Jetson Orin 16GB (100 TOPS) AI compute, 1920×1080 wrist cameras (FOV 130°×60°×160°), a 3840×1200 stereo vision head camera (FOV 115°×80°×125°), WiFi 6, Bluetooth 5.2, a 4-microphone array, 5W speaker, OTA update support, and a 9,000 mAh smart battery providing approximately 2 hours of runtime with a quick-release mechanism and direct power connection option. Total weight with battery is approximately 50 kilograms. The package includes a handheld controller, visualization computer, and full secondary development support with a 12-month warranty. The G1-D family divides into two principal mobility tiers: the Standard (fixed base) and the Flagship (mobile wheeled base). The Standard tier is further divided into multiple sub-configurations (Standard A through Standard E and beyond on RobotShop) that differ primarily in their end-effector type: Standard A (U1): Two Dex1-1 two-finger grippers — baseline general manipulation Standard B: Dex3-1 three-finger hands without tactile Standard C: Dex3-1 three-finger hands with tactile sensors Standard D: BrainCo Revo 2 Basic five-finger hands Standard E: BrainCo Revo 2 Touch five-finger hands with multi-modal tactile All Standard configurations share the same fixed-base body platform — the 17-DOF body with height-adjustable column, dual 7-DOF arms, 2-DOF waist, identical sensor system, and Jetson Orin compute. The Standard A U1's Dex1-1 grippers represent the entry-level manipulation configuration that prioritizes cost-effectiveness, simplicity, and reliability for pick-and-place tasks over the dexterity of three-finger or five-finger alternatives. The fixed-base design was a deliberate engineering choice for the Standard tier. RobotShop's description explains the design rationale: "Mounted securely on a baseplate, it eliminates the challenges of balance and locomotion, allowing users to focus on manipulation, AI training, or interface development in a controlled setup." For laboratory research, fixed workstation data collection, and fixed industrial applications, the stability of a fixed base eliminates the complexity and cost of mobile navigation while providing the full upper-body arm articulation needed for meaningful manipulation research. Design and Physical Features Fixed-Base Architecture The G1-D Standard A's defining physical characteristic is its fixed baseplate mounting — the robot does not move across a floor, but instead provides a stable, high-mass anchoring for arm operations that might otherwise disturb the robot's position during high-force or fast manipulation tasks. The fixed design contrasts with the Flagship's 80-kg mobile base (which includes the wheeled drive system, LiDAR, and depth cameras) and enables deployment at a laboratory bench, workstation, or production fixture without requiring floor space for navigation. At approximately 50 kilograms total weight with battery, the G1-D Standard A is substantially lighter than the Flagship configuration — reflecting the absence of the mobile base hardware — while remaining heavy enough to provide stable anchor for the dual arm operations. Height-Adjustable Column: 1260 to 1680 mm with 500 mm Stroke The 500 mm vertical lifting stroke of the height-adjustable column is one of the most operationally significant features of the G1-D Standard A. With 1 mm precision in height adjustment, the robot can be precisely positioned to match the exact height of a workstation, conveyor, shelf, or fixture — enabling accurate and repeatable arm approach trajectories to work surfaces at any height within the 1,260 to 1,680 mm range. This height adaptability enables a single G1-D Standard A installation to serve multiple different workstation heights by adjusting the column elevation before each task configuration — providing versatility within a fixed-base form factor that would otherwise require hardware repositioning to reach differently sized work surfaces. Dual 7-DOF Arms: 3 kg Payload, 450 mm Reach Each arm of the G1-D Standard A provides 7 degrees of freedom covering the full shoulder-to-wrist workspace with wrist articulation in both pitch and yaw. The 450 mm arm reach (excluding the end effector) combined with the height-adjustable column means the robot's effective working volume covers the height range from 1,260 mm to approximately 1,680 mm + reach at full arm extension. The 3 kg maximum single-arm payload is consistent with the G1-D family's specification across all configurations, covering the weight range of standard industrial and logistics components, laboratory equipment, and service objects within the arm's rated capability. Dex1-1 Two-Finger Grippers: Standard A's End-Effectors The Dex1-1 is Unitree's force-controlled two-finger gripper — a parallel-jaw gripper design with force control enabling it to grip objects at a regulated grip force rather than simply closing to a fixed position. The Dex1-1 is the simplest end-effector option in the G1-D family, covering the pick-and-place tasks that represent the majority of robotic manipulation use cases: cylindrical objects, boxes, bags, packaged goods, and components within the jaw's opening range. Importantly, the G1-D Standard lineup is designed for end-effector upgrades: the same mounting interface supports Dex1-1, Dex3-1 three-finger hands (with or without tactile), and five-finger dexterous hands. Buyers who start with the Standard A U1 can upgrade end-effectors for different task configurations by contacting Unitree's sales team, per the RobotShop documentation. Binocular Head Camera and Dual Wrist Cameras The G1-D Standard A's camera system is comprehensively documented in Robots International's specification: Stereo Head Camera: 3840×1200 resolution, FOV 115°×80°×125° — a high-resolution stereo vision system providing workspace overview and egocentric depth perception for autonomous task planning and teleoperation monitoring. Wrist Cameras (bilateral): 1920×1080 resolution per camera, FOV 130°×60°×160° — the hand-level visual data capture most critical for manipulation AI training. The 130-degree horizontal FOV at the wrist ensures the camera captures the complete contact zone between the hand and the manipulated object from the approaching direction. Technology and Specifications Full G1-D Standard A (U1) Specifications Specification Value Platform Type Fixed-base (Standard) Configuration Standard A (U1) Height (minimum) ~1,260 mm Height (maximum) ~1,680 mm Column Lifting Stroke 500 mm Column Precision 1 mm Total Weight (with battery) ~50 kg Total DOF (excl. end effector) 17 (7×2 arms + 2 waist + 1 column) Single Arm DOF 7 Waist DOF 2 Column DOF 1 (vertical) End-Effector (Standard A) 2× Dex1-1 two-finger grippers Max Single Arm Payload ~3 kg Arm Reach (excl. end effector) ~450 mm Column Lift Speed ~60 mm/s Head Camera 3840×1200, FOV 115°×80°×125° Wrist Cameras 1920×1080, FOV 130°×60°×160° AI Compute NVIDIA Jetson Orin 16GB (100 TOPS) Main Compute 8-core CPU Connectivity WiFi 6, Bluetooth 5.2 Audio 4-microphone array, 5W speaker Battery 9,000 mAh smart battery, quick-release Battery Runtime ~2 hours Power Option Direct power connection (no battery limit) OTA Updates Yes SDK Full secondary development support Included Accessories Handheld controller, visualization computer Warranty 12 months NVIDIA Jetson Orin 16GB (100 TOPS) and Dual-Compute Architecture The Jetson Orin 16GB provides 100 TOPS of GPU-accelerated inference for the manipulation AI policies, visual grasping models, and UnifoLM multimodal interaction that the G1-D Standard A's research and deployment use cases require. Top3DShop confirms the architecture: "An 8-core ARM CPU for motion control and an NVIDIA Jetson Orin NX delivering 100 TOPS AI performance via 1024 CUDA and 32 Tensor cores." The 16GB memory configuration supports the large visual grasping models that modern manipulation policy research deploys. For data collection applications, the Jetson Orin processes the simultaneous video streams from the stereo head camera and dual wrist cameras — managing data recording, annotation assistance, and the synchronization of multi-modal sensor streams that manipulation training data requires. Direct Power Connection for Unlimited Runtime The G1-D Standard A's direct power connection option is an important operational feature for fixed-base deployment contexts. Unlike the battery-limited 2-hour runtime, direct power connection enables unlimited operational duration — a critical advantage for data collection sessions, extended demonstration deployments, and research experiments that need to run continuously beyond battery capacity. Fixed-base robots, unlike mobile platforms, naturally lend themselves to corded power delivery since they don't need to travel across a floor. End-Effector Upgrade Path The G1-D Standard A's end-effector interface is shared across all Standard configurations. RobotShop's comparative table confirms: "Standard: Optional 2-Finger Gripper / 3-Finger Dexterous Hand (No Tactile) / 3-Finger Dexterous Hand (With Tactile) / 5-Finger Dexterous Hand." This means the Standard A U1's Dex1-1 grippers can be replaced with more capable end-effectors for different research tasks — contacting the sales team for end-effector selection as noted in the product documentation. Applications and Use Cases Fixed Workstation Data Collection for Manipulation AI The G1-D Standard A's most directly targeted application is manipulation training data collection at fixed workstation setups. The combined stereo head camera and dual wrist cameras capture the visual observations from both the egocentric perspective and the hand-object contact perspective simultaneously — the two most important viewpoints for training visuo-motor manipulation policies. The height-adjustable column enables the workstation to be configured for any task height within 500 mm of range, and the direct power connection enables extended data collection sessions without battery management overhead. Laboratory Research: Fixed-Base Manipulation Studies For university robotics laboratories studying manipulation, grasping, and arm control where the researcher can control the object placement and task configuration (as opposed to mobile deployment where the robot must navigate to the task location), the G1-D Standard A provides a stable, mechanically simple research platform. The absence of locomotion complexity reduces confounding variables in manipulation studies and simplifies the experimental setup compared to working with a full mobile humanoid system. Industrial Workstation Automation Piloting For manufacturing facilities evaluating automation of fixed workstation tasks — assembly inspection, component sorting, packaging, and quality control — the G1-D Standard A's fixed-base architecture matches the mechanical configuration of existing fixed industrial robots (SCARA, delta, 6-axis arm) while providing the dual-arm humanoid capability for tasks requiring two hands. The 3 kg payload and 450 mm reach cover standard light industrial component weights, and the height-adjustable column enables setup for different workstation heights without reinstalling the robot. Bench-Mounted Research and Education The G1-D Standard A's 50 kg fixed weight and flat baseplate enable secure bench mounting — a research and education context where the robot is fixed to a laboratory bench or table at standard working height. This configuration is appropriate for hands-on robotics education, manipulation algorithm demonstration, and robot programming courses where students interact with the robot at a stationary workstation. Service and Reception Demonstrations For exhibition, hotel lobby, or corporate reception deployments where a humanoid robot provides a stationary interactive presence — answering voice queries with UnifoLM, gesturing with its 7-DOF arms, and demonstrating manipulation capabilities — the G1-D Standard A's stability, visual expressiveness, and WiFi 6 connectivity provide a reliable fixed-base demonstration platform without the navigation complexity of mobile alternatives. Comparison: Standard A U1 vs. Other G1-D Configurations Feature G1-D Standard A (U1) G1-D Standard C (Dex3-1 Tactile) G1-D Flagship Mobility Fixed base Fixed base Wheeled (1.5 m/s) End-Effector Dex1-1 (2-finger) Dex3-1 (3-finger + tactile) Modular options Total DOF 17 19 19 Battery ~2 hours ~2 hours Up to 6 hours Navigation Sensors None None LiDAR, depth cameras Weight ~50 kg ~50 kg Up to 80 kg Best For Pick-and-place, baseline research Tactile manipulation research Mobile data collection Price Contact for quote Contact for quote Contact for quote Advantages and Benefits Fixed-Base Stability for Precise Manipulation Research: Eliminating locomotion eliminates vibration, postural perturbations, and balance computation from the manipulation research environment, providing the cleanest possible mechanical substrate for studying arm and hand control. 500 mm Column Lifting Stroke at 1 mm Precision: The fine height adjustment enables precise workstation height matching across different task configurations, improving manipulation reach accuracy and enabling repeatable experimental setups. Dual 1080p Wrist Cameras for High-Quality Manipulation Data: The wrist cameras at 1920×1080 with 130-degree horizontal FOV capture the hand-object contact perspective most critical for training and evaluating manipulation AI policies. Direct Power Connection for Unlimited Runtime: The power connection option enables indefinite continuous operation at fixed workstation deployments — critical for extended data collection programs and always-on service deployments. 100-TOPS Jetson Orin for On-Device AI: The full NVIDIA Jetson Orin 16GB compute enables on-device manipulation policy inference and UnifoLM multimodal AI without cloud compute dependency. Modular End-Effector Upgrade Path: The same physical platform supports upgrades from Dex1-1 two-finger through five-finger dexterous hands, providing a long-term upgrade path as research or task requirements evolve. Visualization Computer Included: The included visualization computer provides a dedicated display and interface for robot monitoring, data collection management, and development without requiring an external laptop setup. Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ) What is the Unitree G1-D Standard A (U1)? The Unitree G1-D Standard A U1 is the baseline configuration of the G1-D Standard fixed-base dual-arm humanoid robot. It provides 17 degrees of freedom (7 DOF per arm × 2 + 2 DOF waist + 1 DOF height column = 17, excluding end effectors), dual Dex1-1 force-controlled two-finger grippers, a height-adjustable column (1,260 to 1,680 mm, 500 mm stroke, 1 mm precision), 3 kg arm payload, 450 mm arm reach, NVIDIA Jetson Orin 16GB (100 TOPS) AI compute, 3840×1200 stereo head camera, dual 1920×1080 wrist cameras, WiFi 6 and Bluetooth 5.2, and a 9,000 mAh battery providing approximately 2 hours runtime or unlimited runtime via direct power connection. Total weight is approximately 50 kg. What is the difference between the G1-D Standard and G1-D Flagship? The G1-D Standard is a fixed-base (stationary) configuration mounted on a baseplate — it does not move across a floor. It weighs approximately 50 kg and has approximately 2 hours battery runtime. The G1-D Flagship adds a powered wheeled mobile base with differential drive at up to 1.5 m/s, LiDAR, depth cameras, and collision detection for autonomous navigation, weighing up to 80 kg with up to 6 hours battery runtime. The Flagship is appropriate for mobile data collection and autonomous task execution across a facility; the Standard is appropriate for fixed workstation applications, laboratory research, and data collection at a controlled location. What end-effectors does the G1-D Standard A (U1) include, and can they be upgraded? The Standard A (U1) includes two Dex1-1 force-controlled two-finger grippers as standard. The same mounting interface supports upgrades to Dex3-1 three-finger hands (with or without tactile sensors) and five-finger dexterous hands (BrainCo Revo 2 Basic or Touch). End-effector selection for upgrades requires contacting Unitree's sales team per the product documentation. What is the purpose of the G1-D Standard A's wrist cameras? The dual 1920×1080 wrist cameras (one per arm) capture the hand-object contact perspective during manipulation — the most informative visual viewpoint for training visuo-motor manipulation AI policies. Wrist cameras see the same scene the hand is approaching, providing visual observations that correspond directly to the hand's grasp configuration and contact events. This data is critical for imitation learning, behavior cloning, and reinforcement learning policy training that requires high-quality manipulation demonstrations. Summary The Unitree G1-D Standard A (U1) Fixed-Base Dual-Arm Humanoid Robot is the baseline entry point of Unitree's fixed-base wheeled humanoid platform family — providing 17 degrees of freedom in dual 7-DOF arms with a 500 mm height-adjustable column at 1 mm precision, two Dex1-1 two-finger grippers, a 3,840×1,200 stereo head camera with dual 1,920×1,080 wrist cameras, NVIDIA Jetson Orin 16GB (100 TOPS) compute, WiFi 6 connectivity, a handheld controller and visualization computer, direct power connection for unlimited operational duration, and a 12-month warranty. For research teams, industry partners, and educational programs requiring a stable, high-quality manipulation research platform optimized for fixed workstation deployment — without the locomotion complexity and additional cost of the mobile Flagship — the G1-D Standard A U1 provides the foundational capability with a clear upgrade path to more capable end-effectors as research requirements evolve.

products.h1: Elephant Robotics Mercury Wheeled Humanoid Robot X1 (4011300003)
products.description: Elephant Robotics Mercury Humanoid Robot X1 (4011300003) In Elephant Robotics’ own product description, the Mercury X1 is a 19-degree-of-freedom (19-DOF) system composed of the Mercury B1 upper-body dual-arm robot paired with a high-performance mobile base. The platform is positioned to bridge research-friendly openness (software ecosystem support and developer APIs) with practical mobile manipulation capabilities such as onboard sensing, obstacle-aware navigation, and extended runtime for long demonstrations or lab workflows. Elephant Robotics’ Mercury series documentation also frames X1 as a universal hardware foundation for humanoid and embodied intelligence research, emphasizing dual-arm coordination, perception, and 3D machine vision supported by NVIDIA Jetson edge computing. Design and Features Wheeled humanoid form factor Mercury X1 uses a wheeled mobile base rather than bipedal legs, a design approach that typically favors stability, energy efficiency, and repeatable motion on flat indoor floors. Elephant Robotics specifies a maximum operating speed of 1.2 m/s and a maximum climbing angle of 15°, aligning with typical “facility navigation” needs in labs, classrooms, and structured indoor deployments. Dual-arm manipulation and coordinated upper body The Mercury X1’s dual-arm configuration is commonly presented as derived from the Mercury B1 upper body, enabling two-handed manipulation, coordinated pick-and-place, and research into bimanual control policies. The official Mercury X1 page explicitly states it is composed of Mercury B1 + a high-performance mobile base, reinforcing its modular lineage. Integrated HMI and developer-friendly interfaces Elephant Robotics lists a 9-inch touchscreen as part of the X1 specification set, supporting local interaction for demos, quick checks, or user-facing applications. The product page also highlights a “diverse software ecosystem,” including support for ROS, MoveIt, Gazebo, and MuJoCo, plus a Python API (pymycobot) and a C++ Mercury API for lower-level control and integration. Technology and Specifications Core specifications (official) Elephant Robotics’ published Mercury X1 specification page lists the following key parameters: Product height: 1.18 m Working voltage: 24 V Degrees of freedom: 19 DOF Battery life: up to 8 hours Maximum payload: 1 kg Net weight: 55 kg Repeatability: ±0.05 mm Max operating speed: 1.2 m/s Max climbing angle: 15° Storage space: 15 L These values are frequently echoed by reseller listings and industry directories, particularly the 19-DOF structure, speed, climb rating, and 8-hour endurance. Perception stack and sensors Mercury X1 is specified with LiDAR, ultrasonic radar, and 2D vision as mobile sensing components, intended for environment perception and navigation support. Elephant Robotics also lists a 3D camera (Orbbec Deeyea) and a 4-microphone line array with a 5 m / 180° pickup claim, supporting 3D perception pipelines and voice-interaction experiments. Compute and controllers (noting source differences) Elephant Robotics’ official specifications list Jetson Orin Nano SUPER 8GB as the main controller GPU, with 67 TOPS computing power and a 4-core ARM Cortex-A57 CPU noted in the same table. However, some reseller pages describe earlier or alternate controller pairings (e.g., Jetson Xavier + Jetson Nano). These discrepancies are common in fast-moving robotics products as vendors refresh compute SKUs over time. For the most authoritative reference, the manufacturer’s current specification page is typically treated as the baseline. I/O and communications The published spec includes 24V I/O (6 inputs, 6 outputs) and communications options including CAN bus, Wi-Fi, network port, Bluetooth, USB, and serial—useful for integrating end-effectors, sensors, safety accessories, and facility systems. Applications and Use Cases Embodied intelligence research and robotic learning Mercury X1 is frequently positioned as a platform for embodied AI development, including learning-based manipulation, task planning, and multi-sensor fusion. Elephant Robotics’ Mercury documentation emphasizes X1’s dual-arm coordination, perception capabilities, and Jetson-powered edge compute as a foundation for robotics and embodied intelligence research and development. Service robot prototyping and interaction demos With a touchscreen, voice-array spec, and mobile perception suite, Mercury X1 can be used for human–robot interaction demonstrations, interactive exhibits, receptionist-style prototypes, or guided lab tours—especially where stable wheeled mobility is advantageous. The RBTX listing explicitly calls out service robot development and intelligent interaction demonstrations as common scenarios. Automated task testing in labs and structured facilities The combination of 2D vision, LiDAR, and ultrasonic sensing, plus a defined mobility envelope (speed/climb), supports repeatable indoor navigation tests and “mobile manipulation” benchmarks such as moving between stations, object delivery, and structured pick-and-place. Advantages / Benefits Stable mobile manipulation without biped complexity A wheeled humanoid can deliver many of the practical benefits of a humanoid upper body—tool reach, bimanual manipulation, human-aligned workspaces—while remaining stable, efficient, and simpler to operate than full bipedal platforms in typical indoor environments. Mercury X1’s published performance envelope (8-hour runtime, 1.2 m/s speed, 15° climbing) reflects that practical design intent. Research-friendly openness and simulation ecosystem support Elephant Robotics explicitly positions Mercury as aligned with open software traditions and lists support for ROS / MoveIt / Gazebo / MuJoCo, which can reduce friction for labs that rely on standard robotics tooling, simulation-based evaluation, and reproducible workflows. Integrated sensing for navigation and perception experiments With LiDAR, ultrasonic sensing, 2D vision, and a listed 3D camera option, Mercury X1 supports multi-modal experiments spanning mapping, obstacle detection, and scene understanding—common requirements in embodied intelligence and service robots. FAQ Section What is the Elephant Robotics Mercury Humanoid Robot X1 (4011300003)? It is a 19-DOF wheeled dual-arm humanoid robot platform designed for embodied intelligence research, robotics development, and interactive demonstrations, commonly listed under part number 4011300003. How does the Mercury X1 work? Mercury X1 combines a dual-arm upper body (Mercury B1) with a sensor-equipped mobile base (LiDAR, ultrasonic sensing, and 2D vision) and an onboard compute stack to run perception, planning, and control software. It also supports mainstream robotics tooling such as ROS and MoveIt for integration and development. Why is the Mercury X1 important? It provides a practical platform for mobile manipulation—a core challenge in embodied AI—by pairing navigation-capable sensing with dual-arm coordination. Its support for standard simulation and robotics software ecosystems can also shorten setup time for labs and developers. What are the benefits of the Mercury X1? Commonly cited benefits include 19 DOF, up to 8 hours of battery life, integrated mobile sensing (LiDAR/ultrasonic/vision), and a developer-oriented ecosystem (ROS/MoveIt/Gazebo/MuJoCo plus Python/C++ APIs) that supports rapid prototyping and research workflows. Summary The Elephant Robotics Mercury Humanoid Robot X1 (4011300003) is a wheeled dual-arm humanoid platform built for embodied intelligence work where mobility and manipulation must be developed together. With 19 DOF, an official spec set that includes 1.18 m height, 24 V operation, up to 8 hours runtime, integrated mobile sensing (LiDAR/ultrasonic/vision), and support for common robotics software stacks, the Mercury X1 is positioned as a practical research and prototyping system for mobile manipulation, service robotics concepts, and real-world development pipelines.

products.h1: Elephant Robotics Mercury Humanoid Robot A1 (4011300001)
products.description: Elephant Robotics Mercury Humanoid Robot A1 (4011300001) Mercury A1’s published specification emphasizes precision (±0.05 mm repeatability), compact proportions, and a 1 kg payload paired with a relatively long 450 mm working radius, making it suitable for tabletop automation tasks, teaching labs, and embodied-AI prototyping where repeatable motion and a modern software toolchain matter more than heavy industrial payloads. Design and Features Lightweight structure and materials Elephant Robotics lists Mercury A1’s construction materials as carbon fiber, aluminum alloy, and engineering plastics, a combination commonly used to reduce weight while maintaining stiffness for repeatable motion. The company also highlights an ultra-lightweight carbon-fiber shell intended to reduce mass while improving strength and rigidity at the system level. Seven-axis kinematics for humanlike reach and orientation Mercury A1 is specified as a 7-DOF arm, providing an extra degree of freedom compared with many 6-axis desktop arms. That extra joint can expand reachable orientations—useful for teaching inverse kinematics, exploring redundancy resolution, and handling constrained approaches (e.g., reaching around objects while maintaining a tool angle). Harmonic reducer architecture and braking The official specification lists a harmonic reducer as the reduction mechanism and an electromagnetic friction plate as the joint brake. Harmonic drives are widely used in compact robots for their precision and torque density, while integrated braking can help maintain poses and improve safety during power-off states or emergency stops (implementation depends on control mode and application design). “Power Spring” harmonic modules and torque claims On the Mercury A1 product overview page, Elephant Robotics states that the arm is equipped with seven self-developed “Power Spring” series harmonic modules, and that maximum output torque can reach 80 Nm. The same page describes 19-bit encoder resolution and a repetitive positioning metric expressed as 0.6 arc minutes (as published). These claims are typically interpreted as module-level capability and sensing precision; realized end-effector performance depends on pose, speed, load, controller tuning, and mechanical configuration. Technology and Specifications Official specification highlights Elephant Robotics publishes the following core specifications for Mercury A1: Product size: 98 × 128 × 640 mm Working voltage / current: DC 24 V / 9.2 A Degrees of freedom: 7 DOF Maximum working radius: 450 mm Maximum payload: 1 kg Net weight: 5 kg Repeatability: ±0.05 mm Materials: carbon fiber, aluminum alloy, engineering plastics Reduction mechanism: harmonic reducer Joint brake: electromagnetic friction plate Main controller CPU: quad-core ARM Cortex-A72 (ARMv8 64-bit SoC @ 1.5 GHz) I/O: 24 V, 6 inputs, 6 outputs Software and developer ecosystem Elephant Robotics’ documentation describes Mercury A1 as a 7-axis arm aimed at educators, emphasizing a “touchable quick control interface” and application software intended to simplify deployment without complex programming. In practice, Mercury A1 is often integrated into teaching workflows covering robot kinematics, trajectory generation, calibration, and basic perception pipelines (camera- or sensor-guided pick and place) when paired with external sensors. Applications and Use Cases Education and robotics training Mercury A1 is explicitly positioned as an educational platform. Its 7-DOF structure supports lessons on: forward/inverse kinematics and redundancy workspace mapping and singularity handling trajectory planning and smoothing basic force-aware behaviors (where supported by the application stack) Its compact footprint and 24 V operation make it feasible for classroom benches and lab stations. Research prototyping and embodied-AI experiments For research, Mercury A1 functions as a lightweight arm for experiments that need repeatability and fast iteration rather than high payload—such as learning grasp approaches, evaluating control policies, or building demonstration workcells. The Mercury documentation frames the series as supporting scientific exploration and educational innovation, aligning with these prototyping roles. Light industrial and lab automation With 1 kg payload and 450 mm reach, Mercury A1 can handle lightweight tooling and tasks like sample handling, small-part sorting, test-jig interactions, and repetitive pick-and-place operations in lab-like environments. Users typically choose end-effectors (parallel grippers, suction, soft grippers) based on object geometry and required speed/precision. Advantages / Benefits High precision for its class The manufacturer’s ±0.05 mm repeatability specification places Mercury A1 among precision-focused desktop arms, particularly valuable in education and R&D where repeatable trajectories reduce debugging time. 7-DOF flexibility for constrained tasks A 7-axis arm can maintain tool orientation while navigating around obstacles or approaching targets from alternative angles—useful in fixture-heavy benches, crowded lab setups, and research tasks focused on redundancy. Lightweight build and compact deployment A 5 kg net weight and compact base dimensions support portability and rapid redeployment across lab benches, demo tables, or mobile platforms (with appropriate mounting and safety design). Hardware architecture intended for smooth control Elephant Robotics’ product page emphasizes its self-developed harmonic modules and high encoder resolution as enabling precise motion and controlled behavior. FAQ Section What is the Elephant Robotics Mercury A1 (4011300001)? The Mercury A1 is a 7-DOF lightweight robotic arm from Elephant Robotics’ Mercury series, often sold under part number 4011300001, designed for education, research, and light automation. How does the Mercury A1 work? Mercury A1 uses a harmonic reducer architecture and a 7-joint kinematic chain controlled by an onboard controller (quad-core ARM Cortex-A72 listed), enabling repeatable motion, trajectory execution, and integration via standard I/O and software tooling. Why is the Mercury A1 important? It provides an accessible platform for learning and experimentation with 7-DOF robotics, offering redundancy for advanced kinematics topics and a precision specification (±0.05 mm repeatability) that supports reliable lab work and demonstrations. What are the benefits of the Mercury A1? Key benefits include 7-DOF flexibility, 1 kg payload, 450 mm working radius, ±0.05 mm repeatability, lightweight construction, and a documentation/tooling focus that targets educators and developers. Summary The Elephant Robotics Mercury A1 (4011300001) is a compact 7-DOF robotic arm engineered for education and prototyping, combining a lightweight material strategy with harmonic-reducer joints and a published precision target of ±0.05 mm repeatability. With 1 kg payload, 450 mm reach, and documentation oriented toward fast deployment, Mercury A1 serves as a practical platform for teaching modern robotics concepts and building repeatable bench-scale automation and embodied-AI demonstrations.

products.h1: Unitree Go2 Air Quadruped Robot Dog (GO2-AIR)
products.description: Unitree Go2 Air Robot Dog Go2 Series Products (GO2-AIR) Positioned as a lighter-cost option compared with higher-performance variants (such as Go2 Pro or development-focused editions like Go2 EDU), the Go2 Air is commonly used for education, demonstrations, indoor/outdoor mobility experiments, and entry-level embodied AI exploration. As a quadruped robot, the Go2 Air is built to traverse surfaces where wheels struggle—such as uneven floors, slopes, curbs, and mixed indoor-outdoor environments—while remaining small enough to operate in labs, classrooms, offices, and controlled field settings. Its appeal is driven by a combination of dynamic walking control, compact dimensions, and integrated sensing (notably LiDAR-based perception), enabling both entertainment-style interactions and structured robotics learning activities. Design and Features Industrial design and chassis The Go2 Air follows the modern “robot dog” design language: a central body module housing computing, power, and sensor modules, with four articulated legs driven by high-performance joint actuators. The platform’s compact profile makes it transportable and easier to deploy than larger research quadrupeds, while its structural materials prioritize strength-to-weight efficiency—often described using aluminum alloy and high-strength engineering plastics in distributor documentation. Legged mobility and motion behaviors Unitree’s Go2 family is promoted as capable of a broad range of dynamic movements, including walking, turning, and expressive “pose” behaviors that support interactive demos. Unitree highlights software updates and motion improvements via OTA (over-the-air) upgrades, reinforcing the platform’s role as both a hardware product and an evolving robotics software experience. Sensing and obstacle awareness A defining feature of the Go2 Air is its use of wide-angle LiDAR-based perception for navigation and obstacle detection. Unitree specifically markets the Go2 line as equipped with 4D ultra-wide-angle LiDAR for environmental perception and tracking. Technology and Specifications Mechanical and mobility specifications Common published specs for the Go2 Air include compact footprint and moderate carry capacity for a quadruped in this size range: Standing dimensions: ~70 × 31 × 40 cm Weight (with battery): ~15 kg Rated payload (typical): ~7 kg (limit ~10 kg) Speed (typical range): up to ~2.5 m/s Max climb angle (typical listing): ~30° These numbers place the Go2 Air in the category of agile, small-to-mid quadrupeds suitable for controlled environments, light payload experimentation, and short-range mobile inspection tasks. Power system and endurance The Go2 series is widely described as using an 8,000 mAh battery as standard, with an optional 15,000 mAh “ultra-long life” battery in some configurations. Retail listings commonly state endurance of roughly 1–2 hours, depending on gait, terrain, and payload. Connectivity and control User-facing control typically includes mobile app operation and/or a handheld controller bundle, depending on purchase configuration (with-controller vs without-controller SKUs). Go2 Air bundles are frequently marketed with modern wireless connectivity (such as Wi-Fi and Bluetooth), supporting remote command, telemetry viewing, and interactive behaviors. Sensor suite (typical configuration) Many Go2 Air listings describe the platform as including: LiDAR-based perception (commonly marketed as wide-angle or “4D” LiDAR) Wide-angle camera for visual perception and interaction use cases Applications and Use Cases Education and robotics training The Unitree Go2 Air is frequently positioned for STEM education, where its combination of legged locomotion and approachable size allows demonstrations of: gait dynamics and balance control sensor-based navigation fundamentals robotics safety practices and operational planning embodied AI demonstrations (interaction + movement) Its lower entry cost versus advanced research-grade quadrupeds helps it fit academic budgets for classroom labs, robotics clubs, and introductory autonomy projects. Research prototyping and “embodied AI” demos While the Go2 EDU is commonly referenced as the best fit for deep customization and SDK/ROS development, the Go2 Air can still be used for concept validation, teleoperation demonstrations, and human-robot interaction prototypes where full low-level control is not required. Remote inspection and mobile sensing (light-duty) In controlled industrial or facility settings, robot dogs can provide a mobile sensing platform for: patrol demonstrations basic site walkthrough capture indoor mobility testing on uneven surfaces equipment-room navigation trials However, real industrial inspection programs typically prioritize expanded sensing packages, safety certifications, and robust autonomy toolchains—features more often associated with higher-tier variants or specialized editions. Media, events, and interactive experiences Quadruped robots are widely used in exhibitions and public demos, where expressive motion behaviors and app control create engaging interactions. The Go2 Air fits this role well due to portability, recognizable “robot dog” design, and consumer-oriented packaging options. Advantages / Benefits Accessible entry to legged robotics Compared with large research quadrupeds, the Go2 Air’s compact size and broad commercial availability help lower adoption barriers for teams that want a real quadruped platform without building locomotion hardware from scratch. LiDAR-enabled perception for safer motion LiDAR-based sensing improves obstacle awareness and helps enable demonstrations involving navigation, avoiding collisions, and supervised autonomy behaviors. Expandable ecosystem within the Go2 family Because the Go2 lineup includes variants designed for higher performance and deeper development access, organizations sometimes start with a Go2 Air for early evaluation and later migrate to a Go2 Pro, Go2 X, or Go2 EDU for advanced capabilities. FAQ Section What is the Unitree Go2 Air (GO2-AIR)? The Unitree Go2 Air is an entry-level quadruped robot dog designed for legged mobility, interactive demonstrations, and introductory robotics education. It belongs to Unitree’s Go2 family and is typically positioned as the most affordable option in that lineup. How does the Unitree Go2 Air work? The Go2 Air walks using motor-driven leg joints controlled by onboard motion algorithms. It uses sensors—commonly including LiDAR and a wide-angle camera—to perceive its surroundings and support behaviors like obstacle awareness and supervised navigation. Why is the Unitree Go2 Air important? Robot dogs like the Go2 Air make legged robotics more accessible, enabling students, developers, and organizations to test real-world locomotion and sensing without building hardware from scratch. It also supports practical demonstrations of embodied AI, where perception and movement are combined in one platform. What are the benefits of the Unitree Go2 Air? Key benefits include compact size, LiDAR-based perception, strong mobility on uneven surfaces, and a lower-cost entry point into quadruped robotics compared with higher-end research platforms. How long does the Go2 Air battery last? Many published references describe an 8,000 mAh battery, with real endurance commonly around 1–2 hours, depending on speed, terrain, and payload. Summary The Unitree Go2 Air (GO2-AIR) is a compact, LiDAR-equipped quadruped robot dog built for accessible legged mobility, education, and interactive robotics demonstrations. With a lightweight chassis, practical payload capability, and a broad reseller ecosystem, it serves as a widely adopted entry point into robot dog platforms and embodied AI experiments, while maintaining an upgrade path into more advanced Go2 variants for deeper development needs.

products.h1: Unitree Go2 PRO Quadruped Robot Dog
products.description: Unitree Go2 PRO Robot Dog Go2 Series Products (GO2-PRO) (GO2-PRO) Within the Go2 lineup, the Pro variant is typically associated with upgraded sensing, motion performance, and developer-oriented features when compared with entry configurations, making it a popular choice for users seeking a compact robot capable of navigating real-world environments with robust perception and stable wireless connectivity. Quadruped robots are commonly chosen for environments where wheeled platforms struggle—such as uneven ground, small obstacles, mixed indoor/outdoor surfaces, and narrow industrial walkways. Unitree highlights the Go2 family as a “new and improved intelligent bionic quadruped robot,” with a focus on dynamic movement and intelligent interaction. Super recognition system (4D LiDAR) A key design feature is the Go2’s 4D LiDAR L1 perception system, specified with an ultra-wide field of view of 360° × 90°, supporting environmental mapping and obstacle detection in a wide horizontal and vertical arc. Intelligent side-follow and remote operation The Go2 platform includes ISS 2.0 (Intelligent Side-follow System), which is designed to enhance side-following behavior and navigation stability. Unitree describes ISS2.0 as enabling a 50% upgrade in positioning accuracy and a remote control distance over 30 meters, supported by optimized obstacle-avoidance strategies. OTA upgrades and evolving software The Go2 series supports cloud-based OTA (over-the-air) upgrades when authorized by the user, enabling software improvements and feature updates over time. Design and Features Mechanical architecture Aliengo follows the typical quadruped layout: a central body housing batteries, control electronics, and mounting interfaces, with four articulated legs providing dynamic stability. Industrial quadrupeds are commonly built around: High-torque electric actuators (often integrated into compact joint modules) Multi-joint legs enabling stance adjustment and step placement A rigid body structure for payload mounting (e.g., LiDAR, depth cameras, IMUs, compute boxes) While exact joint-level specifications may vary by configuration and revision, commercial technical summaries for Aliengo generally emphasize its industrial positioning, legged mobility, and compatibility with sensor payloads and onboard compute options. Mobility and operational modes Aliengo’s user documentation describes multiple operating modes and gait behaviors, including a “sport mode” variant intended for more agile movement and stair/step handling (with documentation referencing step heights on the order of ~16–18 cm in a specific mode description). This kind of mode structure is typical of quadrupeds that expose both conservative “safe” behaviors and higher-performance behaviors for controlled environments. Integration and expandability A key design goal for research quadrupeds is integration—the ability to mount sensors and run external autonomy stacks. Aliengo is commonly associated with developer tooling and SDK-based access patterns in the Unitree ecosystem, where researchers integrate low-level state access with higher-level control frameworks. Open-source developer resources in the broader Unitree SDK ecosystem are widely used to interface with Unitree legged platforms (including Aliengo in many research deployments). Technology and Specifications Locomotion control and balance Quadruped locomotion typically relies on a layered stack: State estimation (fusing IMU + joint encoders, optionally cameras/LiDAR) Gait generation (stance/swing timing and foothold planning) Whole-body control (tracking desired body motion while respecting contact constraints) Aliengo has been used as a real-world test platform for advanced estimation and learning-based locomotion approaches in academic work. For example, the MUSE state-estimation paper reports testing on a Unitree Aliengo robot and discusses robust estimation under challenging conditions. Other research on simulation-to-reality locomotion also reports deploying controllers on Unitree A1 and Aliengo platforms in real environments. Sensor and compute workflows Aliengo deployments often include combinations of: IMU + joint encoders for proprioception Depth cameras and/or LiDAR for terrain awareness and mapping Onboard compute (embedded GPU/CPU modules) for autonomy tasks These combinations are consistent with how modern quadrupeds are used in navigation, inspection, and robotics research—where perception and state estimation are central to robust movement. products.h1: Unitree B1 Quadruped Robot
products.description: Unitree B1 Quadruped Robot Positioned as a heavy-duty platform within Unitree’s professional lineup, the B1 emphasizes all-weather operation, high load capacity, and expansion space for sensors, compute modules, and task-specific payloads. On Unitree’s official materials, the B1 is presented as a “land” platform intended to replace humans in special or high-risk environments, supporting work in agriculture, industrial sites, and public rescue scenarios. Industrial quadrupeds like the B1 occupy a niche between research robots and specialized field machines: they combine legged mobility (for stairs, uneven terrain, and cluttered spaces) with modular interfaces that allow integrators to add perception, communications, and mission equipment. Positioned as a heavy-duty platform within Unitree’s professional lineup, the B1 emphasizes all-weather operation, high load capacity, and expansion space for sensors, compute modules, and task-specific payloads. On Unitree’s official materials, the B1 is presented as a “land” platform intended to replace humans in special or high-risk environments, supporting work in agriculture, industrial sites, and public rescue scenarios. Industrial quadrupeds like the B1 occupy a niche between research robots and specialized field machines: they combine legged mobility (for stairs, uneven terrain, and cluttered spaces) with modular interfaces that allow integrators to add perception, communications, and mission equipment. Design and Features Industrial chassis and environmental protection A central design goal of the B1 is resilience. Unitree’s documentation describes an industrial protection level and highlights dustproof/waterproof operation; a widely circulated brochure lists IP67 as standard with IP68 optional, aligning the platform with outdoor and industrial environments where splash, rain, mud, and particulate exposure are common. Payload-focused mechanical layout The B1 is characterized as a “super large load” quadruped. Typical published figures cite: Max walking load (payload): ~20 kg (with guidance to keep the carried load within 20 kg during operation) Max standing load: ~80 kg This payload profile targets practical industrial add-ons such as inspection sensor pods, small manipulators, communications relays, or specialized toolkits. Mobility and terrain handling For mobility, common published specifications include: Max walking speed: ~1.2 m/s Maximum step height (stair climbing): ~20 cm Fording depth: ~1 m Together, these figures indicate a robot intended to traverse indoor/outdoor transitions, shallow water, and moderately complex obstacles—typical constraints for industrial campuses, construction areas, and mixed-terrain patrol routes. Technology and Specifications Compute architecture The B1’s onboard compute stack is commonly described as a heterogeneous system: Main control CPU: Intel i5-1135G7 Sensing compute platform: Jetson Xavier NX ×3 This style of architecture separates real-time control and high-level perception workloads (e.g., depth processing, mapping, and detection), which is typical for mobile robots that must operate with low latency while also running advanced autonomy or teleoperation features. Perception sensors Unitree’s official B1 page lists multiple depth cameras: Intel RealSense D430 ×5 A multi-camera arrangement supports wide field-of-view coverage for obstacle detection and navigation, and can help with redundancy if a sensor is partially occluded by debris, rain, or harsh lighting. Battery and endurance The B1 uses a lithium battery system with commonly listed values: Rated capacity: 18,000 mAh Rated voltage: 51.8 V Rated energy: ~932 Wh Charge time: ~1–2 hours Endurance (representative): ~5 hours standing, ~2 hours continuous walking without load (noting dependence on load, terrain, and temperature) These figures reflect a platform designed for mission blocks such as scheduled inspection rounds or patrol shifts, with recharge patterns compatible with depot-based operations. Interfaces and expansion I/O Industrial platforms typically differentiate themselves through integration options. The B1’s listed expansion interfaces include a mix of network, serial, USB, and fieldbus-style ports: Gigabit interfaces ×7, RS485 ×4, USB ×5, CAN ×4 (via an expansion board) Such I/O capacity supports integration of LiDAR, thermal cameras, gas detectors, industrial radios, GNSS, additional compute, or custom payload controllers. Applications and Use Cases Industrial inspection and condition monitoring A core use case for quadrupeds is inspection in facilities where wheeled robots struggle: stairs, debris, narrow passages, or mixed indoor/outdoor layouts. With multi-sensor payloads (vision, thermal, acoustic, gas), a B1-class platform can support: Routine rounds in factories, substations, refineries, and warehouses Visual checks of gauges, valves, and equipment states Thermal anomaly detection on motors, switchgear, and mechanical assemblies Security patrol and situational awareness Unitree frames the B1 as suitable for security patrols and “full scene coverage.” In practice, patrol robots are used for: Perimeter routes in industrial parks or campuses Remote observation in low-visibility or hazardous conditions Alarm verification (teleoperated or semi-autonomous) Surveying, exploration, and agriculture Unitree also highlights agriculture and surveys/exploration. Quadrupeds can traverse uneven ground, rows, and unpaved paths, making them candidates for: Farm monitoring and perimeter checks Terrain scouting and site surveying support Data collection in environments unsuitable for conventional vehicles Public rescue and hazardous environments Industrial quadrupeds are frequently discussed for fire/rescue support and hazardous response—especially as sensor carriers that reduce human exposure. Use cases can include: Delivering situational video into unstable or contaminated zones Carrying small payloads (medical kits, comms relays) into difficult terrain Reconnaissance in smoke, dust, or low-light conditions (depending on sensor fit-out) Advantages / Benefits Legged mobility in complex environments: stairs, rough ground, and cluttered pathways where wheels may fail. Industrial integration readiness: multiple I/O options and compute capacity for autonomy, mapping, and payload control. Heavy-load orientation: payload and standing-load figures aligned with practical industrial tool and sensor packages. How does the Unitree B1 work? The B1 combines legged locomotion with onboard computing (a main CPU plus additional perception compute) and multi-camera sensing. It can be used in teleoperated, semi-autonomous, or autonomy-assisted workflows depending on the deployed software stack and payload configuration. Why is the Unitree B1 important? Industrial quadrupeds are important because they can reduce human exposure in hazardous or inaccessible areas while collecting data and performing routine rounds. The B1’s design focuses on payload capacity and environmental resilience, enabling practical industrial integrations. What are the benefits of the Unitree B1? products.h1: Deep Robotics Lite3 Basic Quadruped Robot Dog
products.description: Lite3 Academic & Research Quadruped Robot Dog (Lite3) In the Lite3 family lineup (commonly presented as Basic, Venture, Pro, and LiDAR variants), the Basic model emphasizes a lightweight chassis, core mobility performance, and developer-oriented “secondary development” support, while keeping hardware interfaces and autonomy features simpler than higher-tier configurations. Deep Robotics markets the Lite3 series as a compact legged system for experimentation with locomotion, perception, and human–robot interaction. The Basic configuration is typically chosen when teams want a dependable baseline for gait testing, classroom labs, or introductory robotics middleware work without the added complexity of advanced autonomy sensors. Design and Features Lightweight quadruped architecture Lite3 Basic is designed around portability and repeatable testing. Deep Robotics lists the Basic robot’s standing dimensions as 610 × 370 × 406 mm and a weight of 12 kg (including battery), which places it in the “carryable” research-quadruped category compared with larger industrial inspection quadrupeds. Mobility-first configuration for training and prototyping Deep Robotics positions the Lite3 family around “extraordinary locomotion” and highlights performance-oriented changes in marketing materials, including claims of increased joint torque and improved control responsiveness for the series. While such statements are promotional rather than standardized lab metrics, they reflect the intended design goal: stable, agile motion suitable for demos and controlled field tests. Baseline perception functions for safer operation For Basic, Deep Robotics lists front/rear obstacle stop and visual following as its perception features. These functions are commonly used for supervised walking demonstrations, basic safety behavior (auto-stop near obstacles), and education scenarios involving person/object following. Secondary development support as a core feature Deep Robotics explicitly states that Lite3 supports secondary development, providing models, a motion control SDK and APIs, and perception development interfaces with sample code. This emphasis is central to Lite3’s role as a research and education platform rather than a closed consumer “robot pet.” Technology and Specifications The following specifications are listed by Deep Robotics for the Lite3 Basic configuration: Dimensions, weight, and payload Standing dimensions (L × W × H): 610 × 370 × 406 mm Weight (incl. battery): 12 kg Payload capacity (walking load): 5 kg Terrain capability and endurance Maximum slope: 40° Obstacle / step capability: 18 cm Continuous operation: 1.5–2 hours Maximum range: up to 5 km Perception and interfaces Perception features: front/rear obstacle stop; visual following Interfaces (Basic): listed as N/A on the US product specification and “/” on a Deep Robotics product listing, indicating a simpler interface set than Venture/Pro/LiDAR variants. Software, ROS, and messaging architecture Deep Robotics states Lite3 provides open SDK/API access and supports secondary motion and perception development on ROS1 and ROS2. Deep Robotics’ public repositories also show how Lite3 development often bridges robot subsystems through network messaging (e.g., ROS-to-UDP conversion and host-to-host transport), which is typical for robots that separate motion control and perception stacks. Applications and Use Cases Robotics education and classroom labs Lite3 Basic is commonly aligned with robotics curricula where students need a real legged platform to learn: gait commands and motion primitives, basic perception behaviors (following / obstacle stop), robotics middleware workflows (ROS topics/services), logging, testing, and validation in real environments. Academic research and early-stage prototyping For research groups, Basic provides a stable physical baseline for experiments in: locomotion control and gait generation, system identification and disturbance testing, teleoperation-style supervision and scripted behaviors, software integration and middleware benchmarking.Deep Robotics positions Lite3 broadly for academic research and reinforcement learning experimentation at the series level, which also informs common Basic use cases even when advanced autonomy sensors are not included. Lightweight site demos and supervised patrol pilots The Basic model’s obstacle stop and visual following features support pilot-style demos such as guided campus walks, supervised perimeter demonstrations, and showcase events where simple autonomy cues are desired without deploying full mapping/navigation stacks. Advantages / Benefits Cost- and complexity-efficient entry point into legged robotics Compared with more sensor-rich configurations, Lite3 Basic is designed to keep the platform approachable while still offering meaningful mobility performance and development access. Deep Robotics’ emphasis on SDK/API access and secondary development is especially relevant for institutions scaling robotics programs across multiple student teams. Portable payload capacity for real research sensors A 5 kg payload rating is meaningful for educational and research payloads (compact cameras, lightweight compute modules, small sensor rigs), especially when combined with the platform’s slope and obstacle performance. Strong baseline terrain performance for its size Published capabilities—40° slope and 18 cm obstacle—support experiments on ramps, curbs, uneven grass, and other “real world” conditions that often break wheeled robots without infrastructure changes. Developer ecosystem and ROS support Support for ROS1/ROS2, plus publicly visible tooling and documentation, reduces the friction of adopting the platform for robotics teaching and lab workflows. FAQ Section What is the Deep Robotics Lite3 Basic Quadruped Robot Dog? The Lite3 Basic is an entry configuration of the Deep Robotics Lite3 series: a compact quadruped robot designed for robotics education, research, and secondary development, with baseline perception features and published mobility specs. Deep Robotics also provides models, SDK/APIs, and development interfaces to support custom motion and perception work. Why is the Lite3 Basic important? Deep Robotics lists 12 kg weight, 5 kg payload, 40° slope, 18 cm obstacle, 1.5–2 hours runtime, and up to 5 km range, plus developer-oriented secondary development support. Summary The Deep Robotics Lite3 Basic is a lightweight quadruped robot dog configuration aimed at education and research, combining published mobility performance (40° slope, 18 cm obstacle) with a practical payload (5 kg) and developer enablement through secondary development resources and ROS1/ROS2 support. With its compact form factor and baseline perception features (obstacle stop, visual following), Lite3 Basic is commonly used as a dependable entry platform for legged locomotion experiments, coursework, and early-stage robotics prototyping.

Technology and Specifications Published X30-series specification sheets (including the U.S.-hosted X30 PDF and Deep Robotics’ official product page) list headline performance characteristics that are commonly cited for the platform family: Maximum speed: ≥ 4 m/s Maximum slope/climb angle: ≤ 45° Step/obstacle height: ≥ 20 cm Endurance: 2.5–4 hours (configuration- and mission-dependent) Range per charge: ≥ 10 km Ingress protection: IP67 Operating temperature: −20°C to 55°C The “Pro” variant is also commonly described as a 12-DOF quadruped (a typical arrangement for legged robots with three actuated joints per leg). Inspection workflow integration A defining technical aspect of X30 Pro deployments is often the inspection stack around the robot: payload sensors (RGB/thermal, inspection optics, etc.), route execution, and data pipeline integration. Deep Robotics’ Power Patrol Inspection Solution document describes an end-to-end workflow: data acquisition → backend access → analysis → report generation → defect warning, positioning the robot as part of a repeatable inspection process rather than only a mobility platform. Applications and Use Cases Power substations and utility patrol Power patrol is one of the most emphasized verticals in Deep Robotics’ materials. The power inspection solution document describes deployments where the quadruped replaces or augments manual inspection routes, with the stated intent of improving efficiency and inspection quality while reducing labor intensity. In such settings, legged mobility is used to handle mixed terrain and access inspection points without changing the environment (e.g., where stairs and obstacles exist). Factories, industrial sites, and pipeline corridors Deep Robotics’ X30 documentation lists factories and pipeline corridors among core application environments.Typical tasks include scheduled patrol routes, anomaly documentation, and condition monitoring—especially where human access is hazardous, time-consuming, or inconsistent. Public safety, patrol, and exploration tasks A separate Deep Robotics solution PDF for public security describes quadruped deployments in security inspection, exploration, and public rescue contexts.For these scenarios, payload choice and comms integration largely determine performance: situational awareness (cameras/thermal), audio/lighting, and incident reporting can be more important than raw speed. Reduced downtime with quick-swap power Quick battery replacement enables longer operational days and faster return to service, particularly when paired with standard patrol routes and spare batteries. Better consistency in repeatable inspection workflows The strongest “industrial” benefit is often operational: pairing a patrol robot with structured workflows for data collection, analysis, reporting, and defect alerts can improve consistency compared to irregular manual checks—especially in large facilities. Deep Robotics X30 Pro is an industrial quadruped robot designed for autonomous or semi-autonomous patrol and inspection, aimed at environments such as substations, factories, and other complex sites. How does Deep Robotics X30 Pro work? It combines four-legged locomotion with onboard perception (described in product materials as “fusion perception”), route execution, and configurable payloads to move through obstacles and collect inspection data that can be analyzed and turned into reports and alerts. Why is Deep Robotics X30 Pro important? It represents a shift from “robot as a demo” to “robot as an inspection workflow component,” enabling repeatable patrol routes in places where stairs and uneven terrain make conventional robots less effective. What are the benefits of Deep Robotics X30 Pro? Commonly cited benefits include rugged outdoor operation (IP67, −20°C to 55°C), strong mobility over slopes and obstacles, quick battery replacement, and integration into structured inspection processes for reporting and defect warning. Summary The Deep Robotics X30 Pro is an industrial quadruped platform designed for rugged, repeatable inspection and patrol operations in environments that challenge wheeled robots. Published materials for the X30 family emphasize IP67 protection, −20°C to 55°C operating range, mobility over 45° slopes and 20 cm obstacles, and multi-hour endurance—paired with “fusion perception” and solution workflows for sectors like power substation inspection and public safety. In most markets, X30 Pro availability is channel-driven and pricing is often quote-based, reflecting the variability of payloads and deployment packages typical of industrial robotics.

products.h1: AgiBot D1 Pro Intelligent Quadruped Robot Dog
products.description: AgiBot D1 Pro Intelligent Quadruped Robot Dog The AgiBot D1 Pro is a performance-oriented quadruped robot developed by AGIBOT for education, R&D, entertainment, inspection, and security scenarios. Positioned above entry-level hobby platforms yet priced accessibly, the D1 Pro emphasizes robust mobility and reinforcement learning (RL)–based gait control, enabling stable locomotion across uneven surfaces and stairs. Public materials highlight headline capabilities such as up to 3.7 m/s running speed, 16 cm stair climb, ~35 cm jump, ~1–2 hours endurance, and 30° slope handling—figures that place it among the more agile quadrupeds in its class at this price point. Design and Features Mobility-first chassis D1 Pro uses a compact four-leg architecture with actuators and control tuned for outdoor stability and impact resilience. AgiBot’s product page underscores self-balancing, anti-fall, and anti-interference behaviors—important for field demos and light inspection work. Reinforcement learning gait control Unlike purely model-based gait planners, the D1 Pro’s locomotion stack incorporates reinforcement learning to adapt step timing and body posture to terrain changes, improving traction and stability on gravel, slopes, or steps. AgiBot cites RL explicitly on the D1 Pro page, and multiple demo videos show dynamic maneuvers (runs, jumps, backflips) that illustrate the controller’s bandwidth. Perception and add-ons The platform typically ships with forward-facing cameras and supports LiDAR and depth sensors for mapping and obstacle avoidance; public listings and demos emphasize environmental awareness rather than high-precision industrial metrology. Configurations vary by seller and bundle, with education-leaning kits providing more open SDK access and peripheral mounts. Weather and durability While the Pro’s specific ingress protection is not always stated in marketing overviews, the sibling D1 Ultra is listed at IP54, suggesting the Pro is positioned for general outdoor use with reasonable splash/dust protection. Final IP/kit contents should be confirmed in a quotation. Technology and Specifications Final specifications and accessory packs vary by region and reseller; verify with an official quote. Top speed: up to 3.7 m/s (running). Stair capability: up to 16 cm step height. Jump height: approximately 35 cm (demonstrated). Slope handling: up to 30°. Endurance: about 1–2 hours (duty-cycle dependent). Control: RL-based locomotion; self-balancing, anti-fall/anti-interference behaviors. Mass & dimensions: AgiBot posts detailed sizes more frequently for the D1 Ultra; D1 Pro is in a similar compact 15-kg-class form factor per media posts and channel listings (confirm at purchase). Applications and Use Cases Education and research Universities and labs use D1 Pro as a mobile legged platform to study locomotion, perception-driven navigation, and multi-sensor fusion. The accessible price and RL gait open projects ranging from terrain adaptation to vision-guided path planning. Public demos and entertainment With high agility and showy moves (sprints, jumps), D1 Pro serves tech showcases, STEM outreach, and live events, where reliability and spectacle matter more than heavy payload. Light inspection & security pilots The Pro’s stair handling and slope capability make it suitable for pilot patrols around campuses or facilities with steps and uneven ground, especially when paired with third-party LiDAR/depth for mapping. For harsher duty cycles or higher IP ratings, buyers often cross-shop the D1 Ultra or heavier industrial quadrupeds. Advantages / Benefits Agility per dollar: RL locomotion, 3.7 m/s top speed, and jumping/stairs make a strong capability mix for the price. Educational value: A practical demonstrator for embodied AI, blending control theory with machine learning in real hardware. Terrain versatility: Anti-fall and anti-interference behaviors support outdoor demos and rough-ground navigation. Ecosystem breadth: The D1 family spans Edu → Pro → Ultra, offering a step-up path for users who later need higher ingress protection or longer runtime. Comparisons (if relevant) D1 Pro vs. D1 Edu: Media posts suggest D1 Pro targets general users with an accessible price; D1 Edu is a higher-priced academic kit with expanded software access and shipment windows tailored to labs. D1 Pro vs. D1 Ultra: Ultra lists IP54, ~1 hour runtime, and ~3.5 m/s speed; it’s positioned as the industrial-leaning member of the lineup. Pro trades some ruggedization options for cost while retaining headline agility. D1 Pro vs. heavier industrial quadrupeds: Platforms like high-payload units often exceed the Pro’s environmental hardening and load capacity but cost substantially more; the D1 Pro’s strength lies in mobility and price-performance for R&D and demonstrations. (Context from typical industrial specs; confirm against project requirements.) Pricing and Availability Multiple public posts and news shares indicate that AgiBot D1 Pro was released on consumer e-commerce (e.g., JD.com) at approximately CNY 13,000 (around USD 1,800), with D1 Edu listed around CNY 36,000; regional pricing and shipping windows can differ and may change over time. The D1 Ultra appears at markedly higher prices via professional channels and resellers, reflecting its industrial positioning and IP rating. For current pricing, accessory packs, and lead times, request an official quotation from AGIBOT or an authorized partner. FAQ Section What is AgiBot D1 Pro?A quadruped robot from AGIBOT aimed at education, R&D, demos, and light inspection/security that features reinforcement learning–based gait control for robust mobility. How fast can D1 Pro run and what terrain can it handle?AgiBot lists up to 3.7 m/s running speed, 16 cm stair climbing, ~35 cm jumping, and 30° slopes, with 1–2 hours of endurance depending on usage. Why is D1 Pro important?It compresses high-agility legged locomotion and RL control into a price tier reachable for educators and labs, enabling hands-on embodied-AI projects without industrial-grade budgets. Where can I buy D1 Pro?AgiBot launched D1 Pro on JD.com at ~CNY 13,000 and promotes distribution via partners; for academic kits or ruggedized variants, contact AGIBOT or resellers for an official quote. What are the differences between D1 Pro, D1 Edu, and D1 Ultra?Pro balances cost and agility; Edu is a higher-priced academic package; Ultra emphasizes industrial features (e.g., IP54) and professional support channels. Does D1 Pro include LiDAR and depth cameras by default?Bundles vary by seller—mapping sensors are common in kits, but exact LiDAR/depth inclusions should be confirmed when ordering. Media/social posts: D1 Pro launch prices on JD.com; D1 series positioning. D1 Ultra listing: indicative IP and performance for the industrial-leaning variant. Demo videos: dynamic gaits/jumps/backflips illustrating RL capabilities. Summary The AgiBot D1 Pro packages reinforcement learning–based legged locomotion and agile terrain handling into an accessible quadruped platform suited to education, research, and public demonstrations, with emerging use in light inspection/security pilots. With specs including 3.7 m/s top speed, 16 cm stairs, ~35 cm jump, 30° slopes, and ~1–2 hours endurance, it offers high mobility per dollar and a clear upgrade path within the D1 family (Edu ↔ Pro ↔ Ultra). For organizations seeking hands-on embodied-AI experimentation without industrial-grade budgets, D1 Pro is a compelling entry point.
