products.h1: PUDU Pudubot 2 Universal Delivery Robot
products.description: PUDU Pudubot 2 Universal Delivery Robot Pudu positions PuduBot 2 as a “universal” delivery platform that inherits core capabilities from earlier PuduBot models while adding upgraded navigation, perception, chassis stability, and workflow tools intended for broader deployment across industries. In service operations, delivery robots such as PuduBot 2 are typically deployed to reduce repetitive staff walking, improve throughput during peak periods, and support more predictable delivery routines. Pudu’s product materials emphasize that PuduBot 2 is designed for multi-scenario “intelligent deliveries” with higher performance and reliability in dynamic indoor spaces. Design and Features Modular, adjustable tray system PuduBot 2 is built around adjustable trays intended to match different carrying needs and venue layouts. Its published configuration details include a tray size of 500 × 432 mm, up to 13 kg per tray, and up to 40 kg total payload (depending on configuration and safety settings). This tray-based approach supports common hospitality workflows such as: Multi-table delivery (serving several destinations in one run) Batch delivery (kitchen → multiple zones) Dish return / collection workflows in some venues (via configured modes) Multi-modal operating modes for service environments PuduBot 2 supports multiple delivery modes designed around restaurant and service patterns, including Delivery Mode, Cruise Mode, Dish Return Mode, and Birthday Mode, with easy mode switching depending on the venue’s desired customer experience and operational flow. Calling and notification tools For dispatching and staff coordination, Pudu markets PuduBot 2 as compatible with Pudu Watch, Pudu Pager, and Pudu Link, enabling “one-click” task assignment and arrival notifications (for example, notifying guests when the robot arrives at a destination). Automatic charging and “standby” operation PuduBot 2 is designed to auto-recharge without manual intervention, returning to a charging station when scheduled or when the battery drops below a defined threshold. This design is intended to support longer daily utilization and reduce staff touchpoints. Durable, enclosed chassis and environmental resistance Pudu’s product introduction describes a fully enclosed machine design intended to better resist dust and water exposure in day-to-day commercial use, with an IP42 rating noted in the product deck. The platform also highlights an “industry-leading chassis system” and improved stability for uneven or bumpy floors, reflecting the operational reality of mixed surfaces in restaurants, malls, and corridors. Technology and Specifications Navigation: PUDU VSLAM+ and Laser SLAM (and fusion options) PuduBot 2 is marketed around PUDU VSLAM+ and Laser SLAM, with product documentation indicating it can support Laser SLAM, VSLAM+, or a fusion approach depending on scenario needs. Pudu emphasizes marker-less deployment (reducing deployment time) and the ability to operate in high-ceiling environments up to 30 meters, along with extra-large scene mapping up to 40,000 m². Dual LiDAR perception and obstacle handling The product introduction highlights a dual-LiDAR system providing 360° sensing, with the upper LiDAR focused on distance detection and the lower LiDAR focused on ground detection (including low obstacle detection). This perception approach is designed to support safe navigation around tables, chair legs, and unpredictable foot traffic typical of customer-facing venues. Core published specifications (from product introduction) Commonly published PuduBot 2 specifications include: Dimensions: 580 × 535 × 1290 mm Machine weight: 39 kg Tray size: 500 × 432 mm Single tray load: up to 13 kg Maximum total load: 40 kg Battery life: 10–24 hours (depending on workload and mode) Charging time: about 3 hours Cruise speed: 0.5–1.2 m/s (adjustable) Minimum path clearance: 80 cm Obstacle negotiation height: 10 mm Climbing angle: ≤ 5° Dispatching maximum: up to 20 robots in one environment (multi-robot collaboration) Charging: wired or automatic docking Software platform and integrations Pudu’s materials reference PUDU OS as an operating system platform and note support for third-party development and integration via open SDK/API concepts. The product deck also describes the ability to safely pass through e-gates or electric glass doors via automated pass-through integrations in supported deployments. Applications and Use Cases Restaurants and cafés PuduBot 2 is frequently deployed in table service environments to deliver dishes from kitchen pass-through areas to table zones and to support dish return routines. The multi-mode design (delivery, cruise, dish return, birthday) is aimed at integrating into different restaurant styles—from casual dining to larger, high-turnover venues. Hotels, resorts, and entertainment venues In hospitality and leisure environments, the robot can be used for internal delivery between service points, and “cruise” or “notification” behaviors can be used to improve visibility and guest interaction during service peaks. Retail and supermarkets Retail applications often focus on staff support tasks (moving small goods or supplies) and customer-facing assistance (presence and guidance), especially where stores have wide aisles and consistent traffic patterns. Pudu’s own “multi-industry coverage” examples include retail and supermarkets among target scenarios. Offices, clinics, and internal facility logistics For offices and clinics, the value proposition is usually repetitive corridor transport—moving items between stations—where staff time savings and consistency can justify deployment in larger facilities. Advantages / Benefits Faster deployment and broad environment adaptability Marker-less VSLAM+ positioning, high-ceiling support, and large-area mapping are presented as ways to reduce deployment friction and improve adaptability across varied commercial interiors. Operational efficiency and labor optimization By automating routine walking and carrying tasks, PuduBot 2 can help venues keep staff focused on customer service, exception handling, and quality control—particularly during peak service hours. Safer navigation and more reliable operation in crowds Dual-LiDAR 360° perception, adjustable speed, and obstacle handling specs are designed to support safer operation in busy environments, where sudden pedestrian movement and furniture changes are common. Scalable multi-robot dispatch Pudu describes multi-robot collaboration and dispatching up to 20 robots in one environment, supporting scale-up in large venues or multi-zone operations. FAQ What is the PUDU PuduBot 2 Universal Delivery Robot? PuduBot 2 is an indoor autonomous delivery robot designed to transport items on adjustable trays in commercial environments such as restaurants, retail stores, hotels, and offices. How does the PuduBot 2 work? It navigates using PUDU VSLAM+ and Laser SLAM (with optional fusion approaches), detects obstacles using dual LiDAR 360° sensing, and completes tasks in modes such as Delivery, Cruise, Dish Return, and Birthday. Why is PuduBot 2 important? It helps automate repetitive indoor delivery tasks, improving service throughput and reducing staff walking, while providing reliable navigation for dynamic, customer-facing environments. What are the benefits of the PuduBot 2? Commonly cited benefits include up to 40 kg payload, 10–24 hour battery life, auto-recharging, multi-mode operation, and marker-less deployment via VSLAM+. Summary The PUDU PuduBot 2 Universal Delivery Robot is a modular, SLAM-navigating indoor service robot designed to automate last-meter transport across restaurants and other commercial venues. With an adjustable tray system (up to 40 kg total load), dual-LiDAR 360° perception, PUDU VSLAM+ & Laser SLAM positioning, multi-mode workflows, and auto-recharging support, PuduBot 2 is positioned as a broadly deployable delivery platform for organizations seeking consistent, scalable service automation
products.metaDesc: Learn PuduBot 2: universal indoor delivery robot with VSLAM+ & Laser SLAM, dual LiDAR 360° sensing, up to 40 kg payload, and auto-recharging.

products.h1: PUDU T300 Standard Industrial Delivery Robot
products.description: PUDU T300 Standard Industrial Delivery Robot The T300 is positioned as a robust, load-bearing platform with an “open architecture” that can be expanded with attachments (shelves, lifts, towing modules) while maintaining a common navigation, safety, and fleet foundation. In its Standard Version, the T300 is typically configured as a flat-top load carrier intended for bins, totes, and pallet-like carriers—supporting up to 300 kg payload and designed for human–robot cohabited spaces where narrow aisles, thresholds, and grooves are common. Design and Features Heavy-duty load-bearing chassis (Standard platform) The Standard Version is built around a load-bearing chassis and a top loading platform intended for flexible cargo handling (e.g., large boxes, material bins, trays, or custom fixtures). Pudu’s documentation characterizes the T300 as “versatile” for industrial transport and “heavy-load delivery,” emphasizing durability and a design that can be adapted to different workflows. “Power-assist” manual push mode A distinguishing operational feature is Power-assist Mode, described as an electric assist system that helps with hand-pushing when manual intervention is needed—such as during specific tasks or map generation. This feature targets real deployments where staff may need to reposition a robot quickly or handle exceptional situations without fully disabling operations. Expandable attachments and role conversion Pudu presents the T300 family as a platform that can be expanded via external modules, commonly illustrated as: Standard Mode (flat-top carrier) Shelf Mode (multi-level carrying space) Lifting Mode (vertical lift interface) Towing Mode (carrier/tug workflows) While this article focuses on T300 Standard, the broader attachment ecosystem matters because many buyers choose the Standard version as a base platform before adding application-specific structures. Multi-modal interaction for factory floors Pudu highlights “multi-modal interaction” components that improve visibility and coordination in busy industrial areas, including: high-brightness indicator lights to show position, traffic-signal-style intent lighting, customizable buttons, and audible alerts. Technology and Specifications Navigation: VSLAM+ and LiDAR SLAM The T300 is described as using Pudu’s VSLAM+ positioning alongside LiDAR SLAM, allowing free navigation without relying on fixed physical path markers typical of traditional AGVs. This is positioned as enabling faster reconfiguration when production layouts change. Pudu’s brochure claims VSLAM+ supports operation in high-ceiling environments up to 30 meters and extra-large scene mapping up to 200,000 m², framing the T300 for expansive industrial sites. Safety: ISO 3691-4 and multi-sensor protection Pudu states the T300 is compliant with ISO 3691-4, a safety standard commonly referenced for driverless industrial trucks/AMRs, and describes a safety stack including LiDAR sensors, depth cameras, collision protection sensors, and emergency stop buttons. The robot is also described as recognizing low-lying or suspended obstacles and identifying yellow safety lines on the floor (a common factory safety marking). Mobility in tight spaces and across floor discontinuities The T300 is marketed as optimized for narrow “human-robot cohabited” spaces with: ≥ 60 cm path clearance (aisle passability) 20 mm threshold overcoming height and 35 mm groove crossing width (for typical factory floor joints, ramps, and cable channels) These figures are central to procurement because small discontinuities and aisle constraints are frequent reasons AMR deployments fail without site preparation. Standard Version specifications (commonly published) Pudu’s T300 brochure includes a specification table with a dedicated line for the Standard Version: Machine weight (Standard): 65 kg Loading capacity: 300 kg Max speed: 1.2 m/s Path clearance: ≥ 60 cm Threshold overcoming height: 20 mm Groove crossing width: 35 mm Run-time (no load): 12 h Run-time (full load): 8 h Dimensions: 835 × 500 × 1350 mm Charging time: 2 h (0% to 90%) Navigation methods: VSLAM & LiDAR SLAM The brochure also highlights a 30 Ah swappable battery and auto charging as core platform features. 24/7 operations and fleet scaling Pudu describes the T300 as designed for round-the-clock operations, citing fast charging and options such as automatic recharging and battery replacement to reduce downtime. The brochure further references dispatching up to 20 robots in one environment, aligning T300 deployments with fleet-scale line-side delivery and multi-route logistics. IoT and facility integrations A recurring theme in T300 materials is “industrial connectivity,” including: elevator control, e-gate access, remote paging/calling, and software API interfaces for integration with existing systems. These features are particularly relevant where material transport spans production zones or requires access through controlled doors and interlocks. Applications and Use Cases Factory line-side delivery One of the most common AMR use cases is line-side replenishment—moving components and bins from storage or kitting areas to production lines and returning empties. The T300’s 300 kg payload and narrow-path positioning are designed for these repetitive routes. Work-in-progress transport In multi-stage manufacturing, moving work-in-progress items between stations is often time-consuming for staff. Pudu’s materials explicitly frame the T300 around industrial delivery processes such as WIP transfer and production-area movement. Warehouse and internal logistics Warehouses and logistics facilities commonly use AMRs for predictable internal routes (e.g., receiving → QA → storage → packing). The T300’s floor discontinuity specs (thresholds/grooves) and 60 cm clearance address frequent real-world building constraints. Heavy-load commercial back-of-house Beyond factories, the brochure notes use in “heavy-load delivery in commercial settings,” which can include large venues with back corridors (airports, hospitals, campuses) where wheeled carts are common and automation targets repetitive internal moves. Advantages / Benefits High payload in a narrow-path AMR A 300 kg payload combined with ≥60 cm path clearance is positioned as a practical balance: enough capacity for heavy bins and carts while remaining maneuverable in shared spaces. Faster layout changes versus fixed-path AGVs By emphasizing VSLAM+ and markerless navigation, Pudu positions the T300 for factories that frequently change line layouts or storage zones—reducing the need for physical track/marker rework. Industrial safety posture (ISO 3691-4) and multi-sensor stack ISO 3691-4 compliance and multi-sensor protection (LiDAR, depth cameras, collision sensors, e-stops) are core to deploying AMRs alongside people, especially where safety lines and mixed traffic are common. Integration readiness (elevators, gates, APIs) Facility integration capabilities can be decisive in ROI. Elevator/gate integration and API interfaces reduce the need for manual handoffs and enable deeper orchestration with MES/WMS workflows. FAQ What is the PUDU T300 Standard? The PUDU T300 Standard is the baseline configuration of Pudu’s T300 industrial delivery AMR, designed to transport materials indoors with up to 300 kg payload using VSLAM and LiDAR SLAM navigation. How does the PUDU T300 Standard work? It navigates autonomously using VSLAM+ and LiDAR SLAM, avoids obstacles with sensors such as LiDAR and depth cameras, and can integrate with facility systems like elevators and e-gates for end-to-end internal delivery routes. Why is the PUDU T300 Standard important? It targets a common industrial bottleneck—repetitive internal transport—by combining heavy payload capacity with narrow-path maneuverability, fast charging, and safety features aligned with ISO 3691-4 requirements. What are the benefits of the PUDU T300 Standard? Commonly cited benefits include 300 kg load capacity, ≥60 cm path clearance, 20 mm threshold / 35 mm groove handling, fast charging (0–90% in ~2 h), and elevator/gate/API integration options. Summary The PUDU T300 Standard is a heavy-load industrial AMR designed to automate indoor material transport with a focus on 300 kg payload, narrow-aisle maneuverability (≥60 cm), and practical site tolerance for thresholds (20 mm) and grooves (35 mm). Built on VSLAM+ and LiDAR SLAM navigation with safety features positioned for ISO 3691-4 environments, the T300 Standard is commonly evaluated for factory line-side delivery, WIP transport, and large-facility logistics where integration with elevators, gates, and software systems is part of the deployment roadmap.
products.metaDesc: Learn PUDU T300 Standard: 300 kg industrial delivery AMR with VSLAM+ & LiDAR SLAM, ISO 3691-4 safety, 60 cm clearance, and fast charging for 24/7 ops.

products.h1: Keenon DINERBOT T9 High-load Capacity Restaurant Robot + Free Delivery
products.description: DINERBOT T9: The Restaurant Operator's ROI Guide to KEENON's Global Delivery Standard The DINERBOT T9 addresses the transport component of this labor challenge specifically, not the full range of restaurant labor but the portion consumed by delivery trips between the kitchen and tables. This is the task with the lowest conversational and customer-interaction value in a server's job, and the one where a robot's consistent, always-available performance can most directly substitute for human labor without reducing service quality. T9 Specifications: The Technical Foundation of the ROI Case The 40-Kilogram, Four-Shelf System The T9's 40-kilogram total load capacity is the direct enabler of multi-table delivery efficiency. Four adjustable shelves with specific configured heights of 19.5, 16.9, 25.3, and 22.8 centimeters (confirmed by Dominion Drones' product specification) accommodate the full range of restaurant service items: The 19.5 and 16.9-centimeter layers handle standard dinner plates, entrée service, and most plated courses for two to three separate tables simultaneously. The 25.3-centimeter layer accommodates tall beverage service including wine carafes, cocktail glasses, and covered hot beverages. The 22.8-centimeter layer handles dessert presentations and specialty service items between the plate and glass height categories. Loading three or four separate tables' courses simultaneously in a single trip from the kitchen dispatch station, then delivering sequentially to each table, multiplies the delivery throughput of a single robot against the throughput of a server making single-table trips. A server making 15 single-table delivery trips per hour, each taking three to four minutes from kitchen to table and back, spends 45 to 60 minutes per hour on delivery alone. A T9 making five four-table trips per hour covers the same 20-table delivery volume while freeing the server's equivalent time for customer interaction. 18-Hour Battery: The Full-Day Commitment The 18-hour battery life is not a marginal improvement over competing platforms. For a restaurant operating from 7 AM through 1 AM, a single charge covers the complete operating day. The smart auto-charging system manages the charging cycle autonomously during low-activity periods (mid-morning, mid-afternoon slow periods) without requiring any staff intervention. This means the robot's operational readiness is never a management task: it charges when it has time, and it is available when needed. Competing platforms with shorter battery life may require battery swaps, scheduled recharges during service, or planned operational gaps that create exactly the kind of unpredictable unavailability that erodes operator confidence in robot deployment. VSLAM Navigation in Practice The T9's VSLAM navigation builds its map of the restaurant floor during an initial mapping session, after which it navigates autonomously without the floor markers or infrastructure modifications that older-generation robots required. The 70-centimeter minimum passage width accommodates the typical aisle widths of most commercial restaurant floor plans globally, and 3D perception handles the dynamic obstacle environment of a live restaurant service: guests pushing back chairs, servers crossing the robot's path, children moving unpredictably, and temporary furniture rearrangements for events. TOD System's European distributor documentation describes the obstacle detection system specifically: "Equipped with intelligent sensors, the Keenon Dinerbot T9 detects and bypasses obstacles from all directions, adapting its speed according to the context. This ability enables it to move safely and smoothly in crowded or complex spaces, ensuring accuracy and stability at all times." Three Deployment Scenarios: ROI in Different Venue Types Wedding Banquet and Event Catering Large-scale event catering, particularly wedding banquets in the 100 to 300-guest range, creates temporary extremely high delivery demand concentrated in a two to three-hour service window. A typical wedding banquet requires delivering six to eight courses to dozens of tables simultaneously, with tight timing coordination between courses. Two or three T9 units with coordinated multi-robot dispatching can handle the bulk of course delivery transport for a 200-person banquet, allowing the service team to focus on tableside pouring, guest interaction, and the presentation elements that require human judgment. The T9's four-shelf configuration is particularly appropriate for banquet service where multiple tables' identical course deliveries can be loaded simultaneously at kitchen dispatch. School Cafeteria and Corporate Dining School cafeterias and corporate dining facilities have the most predictable delivery demand pattern of any food service environment: a compressed peak period (typically 30 to 90 minutes) with very high volume, followed by an immediate return to low activity. This predictability is ideal for robot deployment: the T9 can be programmed to expect peak periods, pre-positioned at kitchen dispatch points at peak start times, and scheduled for charging during off-peak periods. For a school cafeteria serving 400 students in a 45-minute lunch window, a two-T9 fleet with coordinated dispatching can handle the tray delivery component of service while cafeteria staff focus on supervision, food assembly, and the student interaction that improves the cafeteria experience. Hotel Buffet and Continuous Restaurant Service Hotel buffet restaurants operating continuous service from breakfast through late evening are the environment where the T9's 18-hour battery life is most directly commercially valuable. A hotel restaurant running from 6:30 AM breakfast through 11 PM evening service with continuous coverage across three meal periods can deploy the T9 through all three periods on a single charge, with autonomous charging management between breakfast and lunch, and between lunch and dinner, ensuring readiness for each peak period. Fleet Economics: The Case for Two or More T9 Units Why Single-Robot Deployments Under-Deliver ROI A single T9 in a 100-cover restaurant makes one delivery at a time, creating a delivery queue during peak periods. While the single robot improves on no automation, its throughput is limited by a single robot's cycle time. A single T9 handling 20 percent of table deliveries saves a limited amount of server transport time that may not clearly exceed the robot's acquisition cost amortization on a standard payback model. Two T9 units with KEENON's multi-robot dispatching, by contrast, operate in coordinated parallel: the dispatching system assigns tasks to the nearest available robot, coordinates routes to prevent robot-to-robot conflicts, and manages the kitchen dispatch queue to maximize throughput. Two T9 units handling 40 percent of table deliveries in a 100-cover restaurant shifts the labor substitution calculation substantially, freeing the equivalent of roughly one server's transport time per shift. The acquisition cost of a two-T9 fleet at USD $9,800 each (USD $19,600 total) against an annual labor saving of USD $25,000 to $36,500 for a USD $1 million restaurant produces a payback period of six to nine months for a two-unit fleet. Fleet Scaling: When to Add a Third and Fourth Unit For restaurants above 100 covers, or those with complex floor plans requiring longer robot travel times between kitchen and tables, three to four T9 units provide a more complete coverage model. KEENON's multi-robot dispatching supports coordination of multiple units without creating congestion, and the per-unit cost remains constant as fleets scale. Summary The Keenon DINERBOT T9 represents the most commercially proven and economically accessible delivery robot available to international food service operators. Its 40-kilogram four-shelf capacity for multi-table delivery in a single trip, 18-hour battery covering full service-day operation, VSLAM and 3D perception navigation for safe crowded-space performance, and coordinated multi-robot dispatching for fleet deployments have been validated across more than 25,000 restaurants globally. At USD $9,800 with free delivery from Open Bot Solutions, the T9's payback period against the NRA-documented 36.5 percent labor cost burden facing US full-service operators ranges from three to nine months for single and two-unit deployments, making it the highest-ROI capital investment available to the restaurant sector in 2026. Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ) What are the documented labor cost savings from deploying the DINERBOT T9? Restaurant automation research consistently documents 10 to 25 percent labor savings from deployment. Against the National Restaurant Association's documented median labor cost of 36.5 percent of full-service restaurant sales in 2024, a 10 percent saving on labor for a USD $1 million restaurant produces approximately USD $36,500 annually. Against a USD $9,800 T9 purchase price with free delivery through Open Bot Solutions, this produces a payback period of approximately four months. Specific savings depend on the restaurant's service model, existing staffing structure, and the proportion of labor currently consumed by delivery tasks. How does the DINERBOT T9's 40-kilogram capacity create operational value? The T9's 40-kilogram capacity across four adjustable shelves enables loading orders for multiple tables simultaneously, each on a separate shelf level, and delivering them sequentially in a single trip from the kitchen. This multiplies delivery throughput per robot trip compared to single-order delivery systems. A server making 15 single-table trips per hour spends 45 to 60 minutes per hour on delivery alone; a T9 making five four-table trips per hour covers the same volume while freeing equivalent server time for customer interaction, upselling, and service recovery tasks where human engagement creates commercial value. Why is the 18-hour battery important for restaurant economics? The T9's 18-hour battery eliminates the operational management burden of mid-shift robot recharging, which is one of the most common sources of operator dissatisfaction with shorter-battery-life robots. A restaurant operating from 7 AM through 1 AM can deploy the T9 at opening and trust it to manage its charging cycle autonomously during natural low-activity periods (mid-morning, mid-afternoon) without any staff intervention. This operational reliability is the practical prerequisite for treating the robot as a standard service team member rather than a piece of equipment requiring active management. How does the DINERBOT T9 compare to Bear Robotics Servi Plus? The T9 (USD $9,800 purchase) and Bear Robotics Servi Plus (subscription model) are the two most commonly compared restaurant delivery robots in the North American market. The T9's advantages are a 40-kilogram four-shelf payload capacity, 18-hour battery life, lower purchase price for outright ownership, and broader global deployment evidence (25,000-plus restaurants across 60-plus countries). Bear Robotics Servi Plus has documented US casual dining chain partnerships and well-established US retail distribution infrastructure. For US operators whose primary consideration is domestic support and POS integration, evaluating both platforms' specific system compatibility is recommended. products.h1: PUDU Bellabot Premium Delivery Robot
products.description: PUDU Bellabot Premium Delivery Robot BellaBot is often described as a “premium delivery robot” due to its emphasis on guest experience and interaction design (including lighting effects and a stylized “cat-inspired” appearance), in addition to its logistics function. In commercial deployments, it is used to reduce repetitive staff walking, increase throughput during peak periods, and provide a consistent, visible delivery workflow that can be integrated into restaurant operations. Pudu positions BellaBot as a flagship hospitality robot that combines tray-based delivery, multimodal interaction, and 3D obstacle avoidance to operate safely around guests and staff. Design and Features Cat-inspired industrial design and customer-facing operation BellaBot’s exterior design is intentionally friendly and recognizable, and Pudu markets it as suitable for front-of-house environments where aesthetics and guest comfort matter. This customer-facing focus distinguishes BellaBot from warehouse AMRs (autonomous mobile robots) that prioritize industrial payloads and back-of-house traffic patterns. Four-tray delivery system and payload layout BellaBot uses a 4-tray configuration intended for multi-order transport in one trip. Public reseller specifications commonly list 40 kg maximum payload (often presented as 10 kg per tray) and tray dimensions around 410 × 500 mm. This tray architecture supports common hospitality workflows: Single-table delivery (multiple dishes to one party) Multi-table batching (one run serving multiple destinations) Dish collection / bussing support (depending on venue procedures) Multimodal interaction (light, touch, and voice prompts) Pudu highlights BellaBot’s multimodal interaction, which typically includes expressive lighting, touchscreen interaction, and voice prompts designed to guide guests during pickup and improve situational awareness in crowded spaces. 3D omnidirectional obstacle avoidance A signature capability promoted for BellaBot is 3D omnidirectional obstacle avoidance. Pudu states that BellaBot uses 3 RGBD cameras plus LiDAR to detect low-lying and overhanging obstacles and avoid collisions in complex environments (e.g., chairs, table legs, bags, and passing pedestrians). Pudu also publishes performance-style figures such as obstacle detection frequency and detection ranges/angles as part of its safety messaging. Dual-SLAM positioning options for deployment flexibility BellaBot is marketed with an “industry-exclusive Dual SLAM solution” concept—supporting both Visual SLAM and Laser SLAM navigation. This is presented as improving adaptability across different venue characteristics (lighting conditions, reflective floors, crowded layouts, and varying décor). Technology and Specifications Navigation and mapping BellaBot’s autonomy is typically described as combining visual positioning and laser-based positioning (Visual SLAM and Laser/LiDAR SLAM). In practice, SLAM navigation enables the robot to build or use maps of indoor spaces and localize itself for repeatable routes, docking, and task execution without requiring physical tracks on the floor. Sensor suite and perception Pudu’s official BellaBot materials describe a perception stack centered on LiDAR and RGBD depth cameras for obstacle detection and avoidance. The design goal is safe operation in close proximity to people, with the robot able to stop, reroute, or wait when pathways are obstructed. Representative published specifications (commonly listed) Specifications vary slightly across sources and revisions, but commonly published figures include: Dimensions: approximately 565 × 537 × 1290 mm Weight: approximately 55 kg Trays: 4 (with differing vertical spacing by tray) Payload: up to 40 kg total (often stated as 10 kg per tray) Minimum passage width: commonly listed around 700 mm (some sources list other minimums depending on configuration and safety margins) Charging time: commonly listed around ~4.5 hours Applications and Use Cases Restaurants and cafés (table service delivery) BellaBot’s primary use case is front-of-house table delivery, functioning as a “runner” that reduces staff walking distance between kitchens and dining areas. It is commonly deployed for repetitive routes—kitchen → specific table zones—especially in larger dining rooms or multi-section floorplans. Hotpot, barbecue, and high-turnover dining formats High-frequency dining formats (where dishes are replenished regularly) often benefit from consistent, repeated transport cycles. A tray-based robot can carry multiple plates in a single run and provide predictable drop-off behavior during peak service. Hotels and resorts (banquet corridors and dining halls) Hotels often use service robots in large indoor dining halls, banquet spaces, or long corridors adjacent to kitchens, where staff travel time can be significant. BellaBot’s “premium” positioning and customer-friendly design is aligned with these guest-facing settings. Entertainment venues and family-oriented restaurants Because BellaBot is designed to be visible and interactive, it is sometimes deployed where guest experience and novelty contribute to brand perception (family dining, themed venues), in addition to pure labor optimization. Advantages / Benefits Reduced repetitive walking and improved throughput A central operational benefit of indoor delivery robots is reducing staff time spent on repetitive point-to-point carrying. In service operations, this can improve throughput during peak periods and reduce fatigue, while staff focus on higher-skill tasks such as guest engagement, order accuracy, and exception handling. Safer operation in dynamic spaces through 3D sensing BellaBot’s 3D obstacle avoidance approach (RGBD + LiDAR) is designed for the complex geometry of hospitality settings: chair legs, bags, children, and sudden cross-traffic. This emphasis supports safer navigation and fewer service disruptions from unexpected obstacles. Flexible deployment with Dual SLAM navigation Support for both visual and laser SLAM is positioned as improving mapping reliability across different venue characteristics. For operators managing multiple sites with varying lighting and décor, this flexibility can reduce deployment friction and improve route stability. Customer experience and brand differentiation BellaBot’s “premium” positioning is tied to human-robot interaction features—lighting, interactive prompts, and a distinctive appearance—intended to make automation acceptable and even engaging in guest spaces. FAQ What is the PUDU BellaBot Premium Delivery Robot? The PUDU BellaBot is an indoor autonomous delivery robot designed to transport items (commonly food and beverages) on a four-tray platform in customer-facing venues such as restaurants and hotels. How does the PUDU BellaBot work? BellaBot uses SLAM-based navigation (visual and laser/LiDAR positioning) to map and move through indoor spaces. It avoids obstacles using LiDAR and RGBD depth cameras, and completes delivery routes between service points and destinations. Why is the PUDU BellaBot important? It automates repetitive delivery runs in busy venues, helping reduce staff walking distance and supporting consistent delivery capacity during peak periods—while maintaining safe navigation around guests and furniture through 3D sensing. What are the benefits of the PUDU BellaBot Premium Delivery Robot? Commonly cited benefits include a 4-tray delivery design with up to ~40 kg payload, 3D obstacle avoidance (RGBD + LiDAR), and flexible deployment supported by dual SLAM navigation methods. Summary The PUDU BellaBot Premium Delivery Robot is a customer-facing indoor service robot designed to streamline hospitality delivery tasks through autonomous navigation, tray-based transport, and safety-oriented 3D perception. With a widely cited four-tray layout (up to ~40 kg payload), Dual SLAM positioning options, and obstacle avoidance using RGBD cameras plus LiDAR, BellaBot is positioned for restaurants and similar venues seeking both operational efficiency and a premium guest experience.

products.h1: XAG P150 Max Agricultural Drone Starter 7KW 2B2C Kit
products.description: XAG P150 Max Agricultural Drone Starter 7KW 2B2C Kit XAG positions the P150 Max as a multi-role platform for autonomous spraying, spreading, field mapping, and material transport, emphasizing high payload capacity, rapid mission turnaround, and integrated digital agriculture workflows. The P150 Max product page highlights an 80 kg payload, 80 L smart liquid tank, up to 32 L/min standard spraying flow rate (or up to 46 L/min when upgraded to a four-nozzle configuration), and 5–10 m effective spray width with an adjustable droplet-size range. In practice, the Starter 7kW 2B2C kit is commonly positioned as a lower-cost on-ramp into the P150 Max ecosystem—providing the aircraft, control system, positioning/mapping support, and the minimum battery/charging stack needed to begin sustained field work, while leaving room for later upgrades (such as additional batteries, higher throughput charging, or four-nozzle spraying). Design and Features P150 Max heavy-lift airframe and modular mission concept The P150 Max is built around a high-capacity agricultural UAV architecture designed to support multiple mission types. XAG presents the platform as capable of spraying, granule spreading, and sling-load transport, with integrated mapping and modern remote-control tooling for field operations. RevoSpray 5 spraying system Most public descriptions of the Starter kit specify the drone bundled with RevoSpray 5, XAG’s spraying module intended to deliver consistent coverage across a wide swath. XAG’s official P150 Max page lists a 5–10 m effective spray width and 60–500 μm droplet-size adjusting range, reflecting a design aimed at matching spray characteristics to crop, canopy, and application objectives. Included control, charging, cooling, and RTK/mapping components A representative “what’s included” description for the Starter 7kW 2B2C bundle lists: P150 Max with RevoSpray 5, SRC5 smart remote controller, 2× smart supercharge batteries (B141050), 2× CM13600S chargers, a 6kW parallel charging kit, a battery cooling tower, and an XRTK7 mobile station. This set reflects a common operational pattern in agricultural drones: pairing a high-output aircraft with a basic-but-functional power rotation (two batteries) and a supporting charging/cooling workflow to keep missions moving during time-sensitive application windows. Technology and Specifications Key platform specifications (P150 Max) XAG’s official P150 Max specifications and feature highlights include: Max payload capacity: 80 kg Max tank capacity: 80 L smart liquid tank Max flow rate: 32 L/min (standard); up to 46 L/min when upgraded to a four-nozzle kit Effective spray width: 5–10 m Droplet size adjusting range: 60–500 μm Pumps: flexible impeller pumps, 16 L/min per pump, with XAG noting chemical-resistant impeller design and extended service-life claims XAG also presents the P150 Max spreading subsystem (RevoCast 5) as supporting a 115 L granule container and up to 300 kg/min maximum spread rate with an effective spread width figure shown as 9 m. Field mapping and positioning workflows XAG highlights integrated mapping capability and “map-free 3D flight” concepts for field operations, and the P150 Max page references onboard mapping capacity “up to 20 hectares” per flight in its headline feature summary. The Starter bundle’s inclusion of an XRTK7 mobile station aligns with the broader role of RTK/PPP-capable GNSS tools in precision agriculture—supporting repeatable field geometry, route planning, and accurate mission boundaries when operating in variable terrain and connectivity conditions. Charging ecosystem (Starter 7kW 2B2C context) In kit naming conventions used by multiple distributors, 7kW indicates a lower-tier charging throughput class compared with higher bundles such as 14kW 4B4C. The Starter kit’s listed inclusion of a 6kW parallel charging kit suggests the 7kW label is used as a practical “kit tier” rather than a single-component rating, with charging throughput depending on charger configuration, power source, and operating conditions. Applications and Use Cases Autonomous crop spraying at commercial scale With an 80 L tank and high stated flow-rate capability (including 46 L/min with a four-nozzle upgrade), the P150 Max is positioned for large-field crop protection tasks where time windows are short and uniform coverage matters. Granular spreading and broad-acre input distribution The P150 Max ecosystem includes a 115 L granule container and high stated maximum spread rate, supporting use cases such as fertilizer distribution, seeding, or other granular applications where aerial spreading can reduce ground compaction and expand workable conditions. Field mapping and boundary creation Integrated mapping references (“map-free 3D flight” and hectares-per-flight mapping highlights) indicate use cases beyond application: boundary mapping, obstacle marking, and mission planning workflows that feed into variable-rate programs and more structured input management. Entry-level fleet deployment for service providers The Starter 2B2C battery/charger count is often suited to operators beginning with one aircraft, smaller acreage, or shorter daily duty cycles—then scaling into higher-throughput power stacks (more batteries and/or higher-tier charging kits) as utilization grows. Advantages / Benefits Lower initial barrier to P150 Max adoption Compared with higher-tier bundles (e.g., 14kW 4B4C, Swarm/28kW kits), the Starter 7kW 2B2C kit is commonly priced at a lower point (public listings show US$28,900), making it a more accessible entry into the P150 Max platform. A “complete workflow” bundle rather than drone-only By bundling the aircraft, smart controller, batteries, chargers, cooling support, and a mobile station, the kit is structured to reduce integration uncertainty for first-time operators who want a coherent operational stack out of the box. Upgrade pathway for higher throughput XAG’s own P150 Max page notes higher flow rate when upgraded to a four-nozzle kit (up to 46 L/min), and reseller ecosystems offer larger battery/charging bundles—supporting a stepwise scale-up model from Starter to Standard/Ultimate configurations. FAQ Section What is the XAG P150 Max Starter 7kW 2B2C Kit? It is a bundled package that includes the XAG P150 Max agricultural drone plus a starter power ecosystem. “2B2C” typically means two batteries and two chargers, and the kit is commonly listed at US$28,900 by multiple resellers. How does the XAG P150 Max work? The P150 Max is a multi-role agricultural UAV designed for automated field work. XAG highlights an 80 kg payload, an 80 L smart liquid tank for spraying, and modular subsystems for spreading and transport, supported by onboard mapping and modern remote-control workflows. Why is the XAG P150 Max important in precision agriculture? High-capacity agricultural drones can reduce turnaround time in field operations by combining large payloads with automated routes and consistent application. XAG positions the P150 Max around high throughput (e.g., 32 L/min standard spraying; up to 46 L/min with four nozzles) and wide spray coverage to improve productivity in large-field missions. What are the benefits of the 7kW 2B2C Starter configuration? The main benefits are lower upfront cost and a bundled workflow that includes charging and positioning support. It provides a starting point for operations, with an upgrade path to larger battery/charger bundles as utilization increases. Summary The XAG P150 Max Agricultural Drone Starter 7kW 2B2C Kit is an entry-tier bundle built around XAG’s P150 Max high-capacity agricultural UAV and a practical starter charging ecosystem. With official platform highlights including an 80 kg payload, 80 L tank, and 32 L/min spray flow (or up to 46 L/min with a four-nozzle upgrade), the kit targets professional operators who want a complete, scalable workflow—starting with two batteries and two chargers—and the ability to expand into higher-throughput configurations as operational demand grows.

products.h1: XAG P150 Standard Agricultural Drone 14KW 4B4C Kit
products.description: XAG P150 Standard Agricultural Drone 14KW 4B4C Kit In reseller catalogs, the “14kW 4B4C” designation commonly indicates a 14-kilowatt-class charging setup paired with four batteries (4B) and four chargers (4C) to support rapid battery turnover and high daily utilization. A representative listing for the kit describes a package price around US$34,830 and explicitly references four smart batteries, four fast chargers, and parallel charging components sized for “14kW.” The P150 platform itself is positioned by XAG as a multi-role agricultural drone for spraying, spreading, and field mapping, with emphasis on throughput and automation. XAG’s official P150 materials highlight a 70 kg maximum payload, a 30 L/min maximum spraying flow rate, and the ability to support variable-rate application and terrain-aware flight workflows. Design and Features Heavy-lift, modular agricultural UAV platform The XAG P150 is built around a large, foldable multi-rotor platform designed to carry substantial payloads and to be transported and deployed efficiently. XAG describes it as “next-level efficiency” hardware intended for spraying, spreading, and mapping missions. RevoSpray and RevoCast modular payload systems XAG markets the P150 with modular payload systems, including the RevoSpray rotary atomization spraying system and the RevoCast spreading system, allowing operators to switch between liquid crop protection and granular applications depending on seasonal needs. “14kW 4B4C” power ecosystem focus The defining feature of the 14kW 4B4C kit is the inclusion of a larger charging stack intended to reduce downtime. One public bundle description lists four smart “Super Charge” batteries, four CM13600S chargers, and parallel charging kits sized for a “14kW” configuration, along with a battery cooling tower and an XRTK7 mobile station. In operational terms, this bundle structure targets workflows where the aircraft is kept airborne with a tight battery rotation rather than waiting on a single charger. Technology and Specifications Payload, performance, and application systems XAG’s official P150 specifications describe a platform designed for high-throughput application: Maximum payload: 70 kg Spraying: dual flexible impeller pumps with up to 30.0 L/min total flow rate, and a spray system designed for 5–10 m effective spray width depending on conditions Atomization: centrifugal atomizing nozzles with droplet size range 60–500 μm (dependent on operating factors), with high rotational speed spray discs Spreading: a granule container rated at 115 L and a maximum spread rate stated as 280 kg/min under test conditions (granule properties affect actual results) Protection rating and field durability XAG lists the P150’s protection rating as IPX6K, describing it as suitable for harsh field environments and “fully washable” in at least one retailer presentation of platform specs. Charging and battery rotation (kit-dependent) The “14kW 4B4C” naming is best understood as a kit-level throughput descriptor rather than a single-component rating. One public listing for the kit references four batteries and four chargers plus parallel charging components designed for 14kW operation. Separately, other P150 bundles on the market emphasize fast charging with cooling (e.g., “mist cooling” charging stations) and minute-scale charging claims under specific conditions, reflecting the broader P150 ecosystem’s focus on battery turnaround. Applications and Use Cases Large-area crop spraying (liquid application) The P150 is intended for high-throughput spraying in open fields and other demanding environments, with XAG highlighting substantial flow rate capability and adjustable droplet size for different crop protection objectives. Granular spreading (fertilizer, seed, and similar materials) With a dedicated spreading module and high stated maximum spread rate, the P150 platform is commonly positioned for fertilizer distribution and other granular tasks where aerial spreading can reduce ground compaction and cover large areas quickly. Field mapping and prescription workflows XAG’s P150 materials reference mapping-related features such as real-time 3D terrain mapping and prescription/variable-rate application workflows, supporting use cases where application rates are adjusted by zone to optimize inputs. High-utilization contractor operations Kits emphasizing 14kW-class charging and multi-battery/multi-charger bundles are particularly relevant for service providers who operate long days across multiple farms, where battery logistics can become the primary bottleneck. Advantages / Benefits Reduced downtime via higher charging throughput A central benefit of the 14kW 4B4C kit concept is operational continuity: with multiple chargers and multiple batteries, the aircraft can return to service faster than with minimal charging infrastructure. Platform versatility (spray + spread + map) The P150 is marketed as a single platform capable of spraying, spreading, and mapping, enabling one airframe to serve multiple seasonal roles with modular payload systems. High payload class for demanding missions A 70 kg payload class positions the P150 among heavier agricultural UAVs, supporting fewer trips for the same delivered volume (subject to local regulations, safety constraints, and agronomic requirements). FAQ Section What is the XAG P150 Standard Agricultural Drone 14kW 4B4C Kit? It is a bundled configuration of the XAG P150-series agricultural drone plus a higher-throughput charging package. “4B4C” commonly denotes four batteries and four chargers, and listings for the kit reference parallel charging components sized for 14kW-class operation. How does the XAG P150 work? The P150 is a modular agricultural UAV designed for spraying, spreading, and mapping, using dedicated payload systems (e.g., RevoSpray and RevoCast) and an automation stack that supports terrain-aware flight and application workflows. Why is the XAG P150 important in precision agriculture? High-capacity agricultural drones can apply inputs efficiently and reduce reliance on ground equipment in certain scenarios. XAG positions the P150 around high payload capacity (70 kg) and high application throughput (e.g., 30 L/min spraying flow and high-rate spreading), supporting time-sensitive farm operations. What are the benefits of the 14kW 4B4C configuration? Compared with minimal charging setups, a multi-battery/multi-charger bundle can reduce downtime and improve daily utilization. Public kit descriptions specifically highlight the inclusion of multiple batteries and chargers plus parallel charging components sized for 14kW-class charging throughput. Summary The XAG P150 Standard Agricultural Drone 14kW 4B4C Kit is a professional agricultural UAV bundle built around the XAG P150 platform and a high-throughput power ecosystem intended to keep operations moving. XAG’s official specifications emphasize a 70 kg payload platform with substantial spraying (30 L/min) and spreading capability, while the “14kW 4B4C” kit format is marketed around multi-battery, multi-charger rotation with parallel charging components to reduce downtime and support high daily productivity.

products.h1: XAG P150 Standard 7KW 6B2C Kit
products.description: XAG P150 Standard 7KW 6B2C Kit In distributor catalogs, the “6B2C” label typically denotes the included six batteries (6B) and two chargers (2C), while “7KW” refers to the kit’s charging ecosystem tier intended to support rapid battery turnaround and extended field workdays. The kit is marketed as a complete, field-ready package that combines the aircraft, spraying module, power system, remote controller, and RTK positioning support in one bill of materials. Within the broader P150 family, this kit is often presented as a “standard” package for professional operators who want a balance between throughput and infrastructure complexity—more scalable than entry bundles, but simpler than multi-drone “swarm” configurations. Its typical deployment scenarios include broad-acre crop protection, orchard spraying, and other applications where aerial coverage, repeatability, and time-to-treatment can influence agronomic results. Design and Features Bundle-oriented design (what the kit includes) A representative listing for the XAG P150 Standard 7KW 6B2C Kit describes the following configuration: 1× XAG P150 Max agricultural drone with RevoSpray 5 6× B141050 Smart SuperCharge batteries (20,000 mAh) 2× CM13600S smart chargers 2× XAG 2024 battery cooling towers 1× SRC5 smart remote controller 1× XRTK7 mobile station (RTK rover) Although the kit name references “P150 Standard,” the same listing frames the aircraft as P150 Max with RevoSpray 5, reflecting how sellers sometimes align P150 “standard” tiers with P150 Max hardware and the RevoSpray 5 spraying system. Airframe and field logistics The P150-series platform emphasizes a foldable, transport-oriented quad-rotor design intended to be practical for field deployment across multiple job sites. Official P150 specifications cite folded and unfolded dimensional states that reflect this transport-first approach. Precision navigation and safety sensing The kit listing highlights RTK positioning (±10 cm) and 4D radar obstacle avoidance as core operating features, paired with terrain-following behavior for maintaining consistent application height over uneven ground. This combination is aimed at supporting repeatable coverage patterns and reducing risk in complex environments such as orchards, irregular field boundaries, and rolling terrain. Technology and Specifications Because agricultural drone performance depends on configuration and conditions, it is useful to separate (1) official platform specifications from (2) kit-level performance claims. Aerial platform specifications (official P150 specs) XAG’s official P150 specifications include: Protection rating: IPX6K Maximum flight speed: 18 m/s (noted with footnote context) Hovering precision with RTK enabled: ±10 cm (horizontal), ±10 cm (vertical) Rated takeoff weight (spraying): 114 kg; max takeoff weight (spraying): 125 kg (as listed in the official spec table) These official values provide baseline expectations for the P150 platform family and help differentiate it from seller-specific performance summaries. products.h1: XAG P150 Ultimate Agricultural Drone 14KW 6B4C Kit
products.description: XAG P150 Ultimate Agricultural Drone 14KW 6B4C Kit It sits in the class of “high payload” unmanned aerial systems (UAS) used in precision agriculture, where productivity is measured in hectares (or acres) per hour, along with consistency of application, repeatability of flight paths, and the quality of mission data captured during operations. The “Ultimate Agricultural Drone 14 kW 6B4C Kit” label commonly refers to a bundled configuration that pairs the P150 platform with a high-power charging ecosystem intended to support near-continuous operations (typically multiple batteries and multiple chargers sized to keep pace with field cycles). In dealer and integrator naming conventions, “6B4C” is often shorthand for six batteries and four chargers, while “14 kW” denotes the approximate charging power capacity of the included charging solution. Design and Features Airframe and modular payload concept High-capacity agricultural drones are usually engineered around a modular payload bay, with fast-swappable modules that change the drone’s role (e.g., liquid spraying vs. granular spreading). The P150 is marketed as a four-function platform—spraying, spreading, transport, and aerial surveying—emphasizing versatility for mixed farm workflows and seasonal demands. Application systems (spraying and spreading) The P150 is commonly paired with dedicated application subsystems (e.g., spray modules and centrifugal spreaders). In typical P150 documentation, the drone is described as supporting large payload operations (with dealer materials citing a maximum payload in the ~70 kg class), aligning with its role as a high-efficiency platform for broad-acre coverage. Autonomy and mission workflow Modern agricultural drones rely on mission planning software and onboard controllers to execute: waypoint/area coverage patterns, terrain following (where available), autonomous takeoff/landing sequences, and standardized application behaviors (flow control, speed gating, overlap control). These capabilities aim to reduce operator variability and improve repeatability across fields, which is critical for compliance (e.g., label rates), cost control, and agronomic outcomes. Technology and Specifications Note: Specifications can vary by region, year/version (e.g., “P150” vs “P150 Max”), and included module set. The figures below reflect commonly published dealer/manufacturer materials for the P150 family and the 14 kW 6B4C kit concept. Payload capacity and productivity Dealer materials for the P150 commonly describe a maximum payload of about 70 kg (≈154 lb), positioning it above mid-size spray drones and closer to “high-volume” operation categories. In manufacturer communications about the P150 Max, the platform is positioned for high-efficiency coverage in U.S. agricultural use cases, emphasizing output and operational scalability. Power and battery ecosystem Agricultural drones in this class typically use high-discharge lithium battery packs sized for: short, repeatable sortie cycles, fast turnaround charging, and safe thermal management under high load. The 14 kW 6B4C kit format is commonly presented as: 6 batteries (to maintain a rotation), 4 chargers (to reduce charging bottlenecks), plus supporting power distribution/cabling and (in some bundles) cooling or battery management accessories. Navigation and positioning Precision application depends on accurate, stable positioning (often RTK-assisted). Many agricultural drone deployments pair the aircraft with an RTK base/rover solution and datalink to maintain centimeter-level guidance where conditions permit, especially for repeatable row alignment, orchard block edges, and consistent swath spacing. Some P150 bundles explicitly list RTK/datalink components as part of the “non-stop” operational package concept. Applications and Use Cases Broad-acre spraying (row crops and cereals) High-volume platforms like the P150 are typically selected when farms prioritize: fewer refills, fewer battery swaps per hectare, and higher output per operator. Use cases often include herbicide/pesticide applications across large fields where turnaround time dominates cost. Orchard and specialty crop operations In orchards, vine rows, and specialty blocks, operators often value: precise boundary control, terrain and canopy considerations, and consistent coverage in irregular field geometries. Granular spreading (fertilizer, seed, bait) Spreading modules extend the platform into tasks like: fertilizer top-dressing, cover crop broadcasting, and certain pest management granules—where uniform distribution and calibrated rates are key. Mapping and field scouting Some P150 marketing describes autonomous capture of imagery and creation of 3D models or stitched maps for scouting and planning, reflecting the broader industry trend of integrating application and sensing into one UAS ecosystem. Advantages / Benefits High throughput and fewer interruptions The core value proposition of a high payload drone is time efficiency—fewer refill cycles and fewer returns to base. A kit built around multi-battery, multi-charger rotation is designed to keep the aircraft operating while batteries cycle through charging. Scalable operations Bundled ecosystems (RTK/datalink + battery rotation + high-power charging) are designed to scale from a single operator to a small crew managing multiple mission blocks per day, improving utilization of labor and equipment. Consistency and repeatability Autonomous mission execution can reduce variability compared to manual application methods—especially for boundary adherence, overlap control, and speed-based dosing rules—supporting more uniform agronomic results. FAQ Section What is the XAG P150 Ultimate Agricultural Drone 14 kW 6B4C kit? It is a bundled configuration of the XAG P150 agricultural drone paired with a high-power charging ecosystem. In common dealer shorthand, 6B4C indicates six batteries and four chargers, and 14 kW refers to the charging power capacity designed to support high utilization in the field. How does the XAG P150 system work in day-to-day farm operations? Operators typically plan missions in software, load the required module (spray or spread), and run repeated sorties across defined field polygons. Batteries rotate between the drone and chargers, and refills occur at a staging area, enabling steady throughput. Why are high-power charging kits important for agricultural drones? Charging capacity determines how quickly depleted batteries return to service. A multi-charger, multi-battery kit reduces downtime and can keep output high during short application windows (e.g., weather- and pest-driven timing). What are the benefits of using a high-payload agricultural drone like the P150? Common benefits include higher field throughput, fewer refill interruptions, and consistent autonomous coverage patterns. When paired with RTK/datalink and robust charging rotation, the system is designed for scalable daily operations across large acreages. Summary The XAG P150 Ultimate Agricultural Drone 14 kW 6B4C Kit represents a high-throughput approach to precision agriculture: a heavy-payload aerial platform paired with a charging and battery rotation system sized for sustained field work. By combining autonomous mission execution with scalable power logistics (six-battery rotation and four-charger capacity), the kit is positioned for operations that prioritize productivity, repeatability, and minimized downtime during critical application windows.

Modular payload ecosystem: RevoSpray and RevoCast A defining characteristic of the P100 Pro is its compatibility with XAG’s modular application systems: RevoSpray 3 for liquid spraying (crop protection, foliar nutrition, and other liquid applications) RevoCast 3 for granular spreading (fertilizer, seed, and similar granular materials within supported size ranges) This modularity supports multi-mission use, allowing operators to configure the platform for different agronomic tasks without changing aircraft. Premium kit configuration (8B3C) The Premium (8B3C) kit is commonly listed with the following included components: XAG P100 Pro drone airframe with RevoSpray/RevoCast systems 8 × Smart SuperCharge Batteries (high-capacity series) 3 × CM13600S Super Chargers This bundle format is designed around battery rotation: while one pack is in flight, others can be charging, helping keep the aircraft active for more of the workday. Precision positioning and situational awareness features Distributor listings for the Premium kit highlight operational features commonly associated with professional agricultural drones, including: Centimeter-level RTK positioning (RTK hovering precision often cited as ±10 cm horizontal/vertical) 4D imaging radar and terrain radar for obstacle detection and terrain-adaptive altitude control Technology and Specifications Specifications for agricultural drone kits can vary by seller, region, and included options, so it is useful to distinguish between platform specifications and kit contents. Aerial platform (published parameters) A representative published spec set for the P100 Pro platform includes: Rated payload: 50 kg Protection rating: IPX6K Max flight speed: 13.8 m/s RTK hovering precision: ±10 cm (horizontal/vertical) The same source lists typical airframe dimensions in both folded and unfolded configurations, reflecting the product’s transport-oriented design. Spraying system (RevoSpray 3) Key published spraying specifications include: Spray tank capacity: 50 L (often paired with “60L upgraded” kit positioning in market bundles, depending on seller configuration) Max flow rate: 22 L/min (dual peristaltic pumps) Single pump flow range: 0.5–11 L/min Droplet size range: 60–400 μm Spray width: 5–10 m Spreading system (RevoCast 3) Key published spreading specifications include: Granule container: 80 L / 50 kg rated payload Max spread rate: 150 kg/min (commonly noted as tested with urea and dependent on granule characteristics) Spread width: 3–7 m Applicable granule size: 1–6 mm Power and charging ecosystem The Premium kit’s operational positioning is closely tied to charging and battery throughput. Published kit documentation includes battery and charger details such as: Battery: B13960S (962 Wh, 20,000 mAh, 48.1 V) Charger power: CM13600 series (noted as 3400 W with region-dependent input power figures) Charge-time reference: fast-charge figures are often given as controlled-condition results (for example, a 30%–95% charge window under specific cooling/charging setups) Most distributor materials also note that performance can vary with environment and operating conditions and that flight parameters may be measured under controlled tests. Applications and Use Cases Broad-acre crop protection spraying The P100 Pro is commonly positioned for broad-acre spraying workflows where coverage rate, droplet selection, and time-to-treatment are critical. High maximum flow capacity and multi-meter spray width claims are used to support productivity goals—particularly when operations must be completed within narrow weather windows. Orchard and specialty crop spraying Orchard spraying typically requires different mission planning, speed, and droplet strategies than open-field work due to canopy structure and row geometry. Published references for the platform include orchard efficiency examples, reflecting its intended use in specialty crop environments where ground access or terrain can constrain traditional equipment. Granular spreading for fertilization and seeding With RevoCast 3, the platform is positioned for granular tasks such as fertilization, reseeding, and other distribution needs where uniformity and speed can be valuable. The published granule-size envelope (1–6 mm) and spread width claims indicate a design optimized for common agricultural granular inputs. Contractor and multi-site operations The 8B3C format is particularly relevant to custom applicators and multi-farm operations where the primary bottleneck is often charging turnaround rather than flight capability. More batteries and chargers can help a single aircraft maintain consistent output across multiple fields in a day, provided refilling and mixing logistics are equally optimized. Advantages / Benefits Higher uptime through scaled power: Eight batteries and three chargers are intended to reduce idle time and keep the aircraft working longer per day compared with smaller bundles. Multi-mission flexibility: Support for both RevoSpray (liquid) and RevoCast (granular) enables one platform to cover multiple agronomic workflows across seasons. Precision operation support: RTK positioning and radar-based sensing features are positioned to improve repeatability and consistency—important for even application across irregular fields and changing terrain. Ruggedness claims for agricultural environments: An IPX6K protection rating is commonly cited in kit documentation, aligning with the realities of field operation in dust, humidity, and spray environments. FAQ Section What is the XAG P100 Pro 60L Premium (8B3C) Kit? It is a bundled agricultural drone package built around the XAG P100 Pro platform, typically including the airframe with RevoSpray/RevoCast payload systems plus 8 batteries and 3 chargers (the “8B3C” configuration). How does the XAG P100 Pro 60L Premium kit work? The drone executes planned missions for spraying or spreading. The Premium kit’s expanded battery/charger set supports rapid battery rotation—helping keep the aircraft operating longer by charging multiple packs while one is in use. Why is the XAG P100 Pro important for modern agriculture? The platform is positioned to improve operational efficiency through high payload capacity and published application performance figures (such as 22 L/min maximum spray flow and high-rate granular spreading), supporting time-sensitive crop protection and fertilization workflows. What are the benefits of the 8B3C configuration? The main benefit is reduced downtime. With 8 batteries and 3 chargers, operators can better match charging throughput to field pace, which can increase daily coverage when refilling and mixing logistics are well organized. Summary The XAG P100 Pro 60L Premium (8B3C) Kit (XAG-P100-Pro-Premium) is a professional agricultural drone bundle designed to maximize productivity through a high-capacity application platform and a scaled power ecosystem. With published specifications such as 50 kg rated payload, 22 L/min dual-pump spraying, 60–400 μm droplet control, and 150 kg/min granular spreading—and with an included set of eight batteries and three chargers—the Premium kit is positioned for operators who prioritize uptime, multi-mission flexibility, and repeatable precision in time-sensitive field operations.

products.h1: XAG P150 Max Agricultural Drone With Revospray 5 (09-007-00158)
products.description: XAG P150 Max Agricultural Drone With Revospray 5 (09-007-00158) It is positioned as a flagship “large-format” spraying platform in XAG’s product lineup, emphasizing an 80-liter smart liquid tank, high spray flow, and sensor-driven autonomy for operation in broad-acre fields, orchards, and uneven terrain. In sales channels, the aircraft is frequently offered either as a drone-only configuration (aircraft bundled with the RevoSpray 5 spraying system) or as part of multi-battery “kits” that add charging, cooling, and RTK accessories to support continuous work cycles. A common reference configuration includes the P150 Max with RevoSpray 5 (09-007-00158) as the core item within larger bundles. Design and Features Airframe and heavy-lift configuration The P150 Max uses a quad-rotor airframe engineered for high payload operation and field durability, with published propulsion specifications showing four motors rated at 4850 W each and a foldable propeller system using carbon-fiber composite materials. XAG markets the platform as capable of covering large areas by reducing refills and improving flight efficiency, citing an 80 kg payload capacity and a maximum flight speed up to 20 m/s in product messaging. RevoSpray 5 spraying system The RevoSpray 5 system is the defining payload for the 09-007-00158 configuration. Manufacturer materials describe: 80 L smart liquid tank Dual centrifugal atomizing nozzles Maximum flow rate of 32 L/min in the standard two-nozzle configuration Optional four-nozzle upgrade that increases the flow rate to 46 L/min The RevoSpray 5 design is presented as aiming for higher productivity per flight while supporting different application goals (coverage, penetration, drift control) through adjustable droplet parameters. Sensing, autonomy, and precision positioning XAG positions the P150 family around autonomous operation enabled by advanced sensing: 4D imaging radar obstacle detection, published as detecting obstacles along the flight path from 1.5 to 100 meters (P150 family feature set) RTK-enabled hovering/positioning support is commonly cited in product literature and dealer spec tables as ±10 cm (horizontal/vertical), supporting repeatable flight lines and consistent swath spacing in precision application work. Technology and Specifications Because “drone-only” and “kit” offerings vary by dealer, the most useful approach is to separate aircraft + RevoSpray 5 specs from operational bundles. Core aircraft and propulsion (manufacturer specs) Published P150 Max specifications include: Motor quantity: 4 Rated power (single motor): 4850 W These figures situate the P150 Max in the heavy-lift segment of agricultural UAVs where propulsion reserves are designed to support large liquid payloads and stable low-altitude flight. RevoSpray 5 (manufacturer specs and published highlights) Manufacturer and reputable third-party summaries commonly cite: Rated tank capacity: 80 L Maximum payload (spray): 80 kg Atomized droplet size range: 60–500 μm Effective spray width: 5–10 m Max flow rate: 32 L/min (standard), 46 L/min (with four-nozzle kit) XAG also describes the pumps and nozzle system in reliability terms (e.g., pump design and service-life goals) as part of the platform’s “always-ready” positioning for seasonal peaks. Operational ecosystem: batteries, charging, and cooling (typical kit context) While 09-007-00158 refers to the P150 Max with RevoSpray 5, it is commonly embedded within bundles that add batteries, chargers, and cooling towers. For example, a “Standard 7kW 6B2C” configuration lists the same 09-007-00158 aircraft paired with six batteries, two chargers, and battery cooling towers, plus an RTK rover station and a smart controller. This structure reflects a core reality of high-capacity ag drones: daily output often depends as much on charging throughput and battery rotation as on flight performance. Applications and Use Cases Broad-acre crop protection spraying In broad-acre operations, high-capacity drones are typically deployed when timing is critical—such as pest outbreaks, fungicide windows, or post-rain accessibility issues. The P150 Max’s 80 L tank and high flow rates are designed to reduce the number of refills and enable larger continuous treatment blocks under appropriate conditions. Orchards and dense canopies XAG and industry coverage frequently frame the P150 Max as suitable for orchards and dense canopies, where droplet penetration and coverage can be challenging. The optional four-nozzle upgrade (46 L/min) is specifically promoted for thorough coverage in dense orchards. Uneven terrain and hard-to-access areas The P150 product family highlights radar-based obstacle detection and terrain-aware operation as a way to support safe flight around obstacles and across uneven ground—useful where ground machinery is constrained by slope, wet soils, or narrow access. Professional contractors and multi-site spraying crews For contractors, fleet economics often prioritize utilization: keeping aircraft airborne during daylight windows and minimizing turnaround between sorties. In dealer bundles that include cooling and fast-charging infrastructure, the P150 Max is positioned as a commercial tool intended for sustained daily output. Advantages / Benefits High productivity per sortie: An 80 L tank and high maximum flow rate support larger treatment blocks per flight and can reduce operational overhead tied to refills and mixing logistics. Adaptability for different agronomic goals: Adjustable droplet size (60–500 μm) and multi-nozzle configurations allow operators to tune spray characteristics for different crops and application objectives, subject to label requirements and local regulations. Sensor-driven autonomy and safety support: 4D imaging radar (published detection ranges in the P150 family literature) supports obstacle-aware operation and reduces dependence on ambient light for sensing compared to vision-only systems. Scalable operations via accessories and kits: The same aircraft SKU (09-007-00158) is used as a building block within multi-battery kits, allowing operators to scale for longer workdays by investing in power infrastructure. FAQ Section What is the XAG P150 Max Agricultural Drone with RevoSpray 5 (09-007-00158)? It is a heavy-payload agricultural spraying drone configuration combining the XAG P150 Max aircraft with the RevoSpray 5 spraying system, centered on an 80 L smart liquid tank and high-flow centrifugal nozzle spraying. How does the XAG P150 Max work? The drone flies planned routes over fields at low altitude, using onboard autonomy features and sensing to maintain stable operation. RevoSpray 5 meters liquid from the tank through pumps to centrifugal atomizing nozzles, producing droplets across a published adjustable range and delivering a high maximum flow rate, depending on nozzle configuration. Why is the XAG P150 Max important? It targets high-throughput crop protection by combining a large tank (80 L) with high spray flow (32 L/min standard; 46 L/min with a four-nozzle upgrade) and sensor-driven autonomy aimed at safe, repeatable operation across large areas and challenging terrain. What are the benefits of the XAG P150 Max with RevoSpray 5? Commonly cited benefits include higher productivity per flight from the 80 L tank, high flow rates for time-sensitive spraying, adjustable droplet sizing, and radar-supported sensing for obstacle-aware operation. Summary The XAG P150 Max Agricultural Drone with RevoSpray 5 (09-007-00158) is a high-capacity agricultural UAV designed for professional crop protection workflows, combining an 80 L smart tank, high published spray throughput (32 L/min, up to 46 L/min with a four-nozzle upgrade), and autonomy-oriented sensing such as 4D imaging radar and RTK-supported precision. As a “drone-only” core unit and as the foundation of multi-battery commercial kits, it is positioned for operators who prioritize daily uptime, repeatable application quality, and large-area productivity in modern precision agriculture.
